text: **The Record of Lost and Forgotten Things** 〔Compiled by Wang Jia of the Former Qin Dynasty〕 〔Edited by Xiao Qi of the Southern Dynasty, Liang〕 **Table of Contents** **Preface** **Volume One** - **Spring Emperor Fuxi** - **Flame Emperor Shennong** - **Yellow Emperor Xuan Yuan** - **Shao Hao** - **Zhuan Xu** - **Emperor Ku** - **Emperor Yao** - **Emperor Shun** **Volume Two** - **Yu the Great** - **King Tang of Shang** - **Zhou Dynasty** **Volume Three** - **King Mu of Zhou** - **Duke Xi of Lu** - **King Ling of Zhou** **Volume Four** - **King Zhao of Yan (Five Events)** - **Qin Shi Huang (Four Events)** **Volume Five** - **Early Han Dynasty** **Volume Six** - **Late Han Dynasty** - **Eastern Han Dynasty** **Volume Seven** - **State of Wei** **Volume Eight** - **State of Wu**

**Shu** **Chapter Nine** **Matters of the Jin Dynasty** **Chapter Ten** **Various Famous Mountains** **Mount Kunlun** **Penglai Mountain** **Fangzhang Mountain** **Yingzhou** **Yuanqiao Mountain** **Daiyu Mountain** **Kunwu Mountain** **Dongting Mountain** **Preface** The *Shiyi Ji* is a work by Wang Jia, courtesy name Zini, from Anyang, Longxi, during the Jin Dynasty. It consists of nineteen volumes and two hundred and twenty chapters, all of which are incomplete. During the late period of the false Qin, the imperial authority was displaced, and the five capitals were destroyed. The land of the Yellow and Luo rivers became a barbarian wasteland, palaces and houses were overgrown with weeds, and the imperial library was destroyed. Thorns and frost, how can they only sadden the former kings; fields turned into grain, how much more sorrowful for this age! Thus, the imperial decrees and records were scattered and destroyed, schools and academies were burned to ashes, and the imperial records and charts were almost entirely lost. Therefore, this book has many missing parts. The text begins from the era of Fuxi and Yan Emperor and ends with the late Western Jin Dynasty. The five dynastic cycles continued, spanning fourteen generations. Zini Wang compiled the differences and similarities, and all unusual and strange matters were included. The record of events is simple and straightforward, loving breadth and valuing the strange. It follows the ancient rules of writing and combines various texts. What is not recorded in the *Shanhaijing* and what is not preserved in the Xia tripod. Thus, it was collected and recorded. The language is overly fantastical, and the meaning is remote and broad. Explaining the reasons and recounting the past, it is regrettable for its verbosity. It often involves books of auspicious signs and widely collects stories of immortals. It speaks of all things in a marvelous way, truly extraordinary and extensive.

The Decline of Ancestral Virtue and the Incompleteness of the Text The decline of ancestral virtue and the incompleteness of the text are evident. Qi further pruned the complexity and disorder, recording its true merits. He searched out and published hidden secrets, gathered and collected scattered remnants. The words are not frivolous or bizarre, and the matters are not false or slanderous. By carefully examining past events, they penetrate the classics and histories. By testing the true and strange, they align with the records and documents. For those whose way of life is distant, the words are concise and simple; for those whose family virtue is recent, the text retains its ornate beauty. By compiling words and connecting them to objects, they form a coherent and elegant chapter. The fate is aligned with the times, and the customs and policies change with the times. As for ancient scripts resembling bird patterns and inscriptions resembling insect patterns, they have been passed down through later generations, often deviating from the original traces. Even though studied and engraved, they are still often doubted and mistaken. When it comes to politics and governance, the signs of good fortune are often false. They are arranged according to the dynasties. The regions of land and mountains, sometimes confused by names and examples; the plants, animals, and birds, also confused by sounds and appearances. They are distinguished according to what is recorded and explained according to the local context. Sometimes adapted to fit the path, it is not possible to adopt a single opinion. Now searching for and collecting the remnants, combined into one volume, a total of ten volumes, preface and record it.

Flora Ts'ui The Lost Records, Volume One Spring Emperor Fuxi The Spring Emperor is another name for Fuxi. The country he ruled had a place called Huaxu Island. The divine mother walked upon it, and a green rainbow encircled her, lasting for a long time before fading away. She then became pregnant, and after twelve years, she gave birth to Fuxi. Fuxi had a long head and long eyes, tortoise teeth and dragon lips, white hair on his eyebrows, and a beard that hung down to the ground. Some said: Jupiter completes one orbit around the sky every twelve years, and this aligns with the timing of his birth. Moreover, it is said that when a sage is born, there are always auspicious signs. In ancient times, Renhuang had a snake body with nine heads, since the beginning of creation. At that time, the sun and moon had double wheels, and the mountains were bright and the seas were calm. Since then, mountains have become valleys and valleys have become mountains, and the world has undergone changes over time, which are difficult to calculate. Compared to his sage virtue, he surpasses previous emperors. From this point, rites, morality, and cultural artifacts were established. He abandoned cave-dwelling and changed from eating raw meat. He established rites and teachings to guide culture and created weapons to adorn martial arts. He made silk and mulberry trees into zithers and clay into ocarinas.

The rites and music were thus established. He harmonized the eight winds, drew the eight trigrams, divided into six positions to correct the six ancestral spirits. At that time, there were no written records; he measured the heavens and drew diagrams, squared the earth and took its measure, observed the patterns of the five planets, and divided the degrees of the sun's shadow, summoned the spirits to all the shrines, examined the terrain to determine the rivers and mountains, and began marriage to cultivate human relationships. The term "庖" (庖) means "to contain," indicating that he encompassed all things. He offered sacrifices to all the gods, and the people revered him as a sage, hence the name *Paotse* (庖牺), also called *Fuxi* (伏羲). He transformed the chaotic state and civilized and educated, hence the name *Mifuxi* (宓牺). He spread great virtue throughout the world, and all beings, without exception, revered him. He ruled with the virtue of wood, hence the name *Spring Emperor* (春皇). His wisdom and insight illuminated the eight regions, this is called *Taihao* (太昊). Taihao means "bright." He occupied the eastern position, nurturing and cultivating the crude and unformed, in harmony with the virtue of wood, his music was associated with the *jiao* (角) pitch, hence the title *Wood Emperor* (木皇).

Emperor Yan, the Divine Farmer Emperor Yan began teaching the people to use plows, personally working diligently in the fields. All grains flourished abundantly. The influence of his virtuous nature was such that nothing remained unaffected. Divine mushrooms displayed their unusual colors, and spiritual plants sprouted their fine grains. On land, red lotuses grew in pairs like umbrellas, and fragrant dew dripped, flowing down to form a pool, which became a garden for raising dragons. Red grass spread along the streets, and purple clouds gathered in the dense vegetation. He built a circular altar to worship the morning sun and adorned the jade steps to welcome the night light. He played harmonious music of the nine heavens, and all beasts danced in unison. The eight tones were perfectly harmonized, and wood and stone became moist and lustrous. Sometimes clouds flowed and scattered dew, which was called "dawn nectar." Whoever consumed it attained the Dao and aged later than others. There was a jade called "Night Brightness" from the Stone Lian mountain, which, when thrown into water in the dark, floated without sinking. During this time, the simplicity of the era of Paoxi began to change, and the use of cultural artifacts became more apparent. Sometimes a red bird carried a stalk of nine-grain rice; when it fell to the ground, the emperor picked it up and planted it in the fields; those who ate it did not age. Copper was collected from the Juhnhan mountain to make utensils. Juhnhan is a mountain, and below it was a golden well with white vapor rising above it. When people ascended into it, the sound of thunder was heard underground. The gold in the well was soft and could be sealed.

**Translation:** In recording, it is carefully noted according to the *Book of Changes* that Fu Xi, the ancient sage, observed patterns in the heavens and understood principles on earth. He looked up at the heavens and down at the earth, weaving together all things, and his highest virtue was fully realized in the mysterious and unseen aspects of the universe. The divine transformation was connected to the finest and most refined elements of existence. Thus, the *Book of Changes* and the *Lo Shu Square* recorded his traces, and the Yellow River and the Luo River expressed their texts. He transformed the primitive and simple nature of the world and changed its distant and remote transformations. The three realms were established, and the four cardinal directions were extended. Rituals, music, and cultural artifacts originated from this time. These traditions were passed down through generations and gradually adapted over time. The *Eight Diagrams* recorded their distant tracks, and the *Nine Provinces* documented their simple transformations. These records were meticulously documented and compiled by historians. After examining ancient records, these traditions were established. They were recorded in official documents and verified by various historical sources. Scholars gathered information from many sources to find detailed and verifiable evidence. According to the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, it is said: "On Tangdi Mountain, there is floating water jade. In the Wujiu region, the trees have many patterns." Unless the true path is simple and unadorned, and it aligns with deep, hidden truths, and is in harmony with the four seasons and the spirits, and shares the same virtues, how could such auspicious signs and wonders come about? Therefore, because of sincere actions, even the hidden spirits reveal their treasures, and the spirits cut away harm, and even different kinds of beings will become tamed. By transforming dew into a pool and housing dragons in a garden, by the time of the Xia Dynasty, the lineage had continued for many generations. There were officials responsible for taming or raising dragons. After examining ancient records, this tradition was established.

The Yellow Emperor, Xuanyuan The Yellow Emperor, Xuanyuan, emerged from the state of Youxiong. His mother was named Hao Shu, and he was born on a day associated with the earth element, hence he was called the king of the earth virtue. At the time, there was an auspicious sign of a yellow star. He established the calendar and began to create written characters. Wearing a ceremonial cap and draping a robe, he was thus praised in songs of imperial grandeur. Transforming the use of rafts into the creation of boats, aquatic creatures leaped in an auspicious manner, and the vast sea became calm. Floating on the river and sinking a jade disc, marsh horses called out in groups, and mountain vehicles filled the fields. Playing the jade flute, he corrected the celestial balance. He appointed four historians to oversee records and documents, and entrusted officials categorized by nine virtues to govern the myriad states. The nine categories were: filial piety, kindness, literature, trustworthiness, speech, loyalty, respect, bravery, and righteousness. He observed heaven and earth, and worshiped myriad spirits, also serving as a minister of the nine virtues. When the warm, fragrant wind arrived, true persons gathered, and he left the world from Kunlun Terrace, leaving behind his crown, sword, belt, and shoes. Kunlun Terrace is the most elevated place in the mythical region of Dinghu, where a hall was built beneath it. The emperor rode a cloud dragon and traveled. In distant lands and remote regions, people still look towards and worship him. The emperor cast objects from divine metal, all inscribed with engravings.

After his passing, the ministers observed his inscription, which was filled with ancient characters, most of which had worn away or were missing. All that he had constructed or established were inscribed with the year and time, and his words and deeds were all simple. The emperor decreed that all the princes and ministers who had received his moral teachings should first arrange jade on a mat of fragrant grass, burn the fragrant wood of the sandalwood tree, grind various precious materials into powder, mix them with the glue of sandalwood to make a paste, and apply it to the ground, thereby distinguishing the positions of the high and low, the Chinese and the foreigners. [This event is recorded in *The Book of Fengshan*.] The emperor ordered Fenghou to carry the books and Changbo to bear the sword, and they traveled in the morning along the Huan River and returned in the evening to Yinpu, covering ten thousand miles in a single breath. The Huan River was like sand and dust, and stepping on it caused one to sink, its depth being hard to measure. Strong winds blew sand like mist, and there were many divine dragons, fish, and turtles, all of which could fly. There were stone lotuses of a bluish color, hard yet very light, swaying with the wind and covering the surface of the waves, with one stem bearing a hundred leaves and blooming once every thousand years. This place is also known as "Shalan," meaning that the sand rises to form waves. The immortal Ningfeng ate flying fish and died, then was reborn after two hundred years, hence Mr. Ning composed a seven-character ode while traveling in the desert sea: "The blue lotus shines for a thousand years, and a hundred years is but a temporary death when eating flying fish." Thus, this is the lotus and the fish.

Shao Hao Shao Hao reigned with the virtue of metal. His mother was called Huang'e, who lived in a palace of jade and wove at night. Sometimes she would ride a raft made of wood and travel during the day, passing through the misty shores of Qiong Sang. At that time, there was a divine child with an extraordinary appearance, known as the son of the White Emperor, the essence of the planet Venus. He descended to the water's edge and played with Huang'e, playing (女更)juan music, wandering and forgetting to return. Qiong Sang is on the western sea, where there is a solitary mulberry tree reaching thousands of feet high, with red leaves and purple fruits, bearing fruit once every ten thousand years. Eating its fruit allows one to live beyond the heavens and age. The divine child and Huang'e sailed on the sea, using cinnamon branches as a flagpole and incense grass as a banner, carving a jade magpie and placing it at the top of the flag, saying the magpie knows the signs of the four seasons, hence the *Spring and Autumn Annals* says "Si Zhi," which is so. The modern wind vane is a remnant of that. The divine child and Huang'e sat together, playing a zither made of paulownia and catalpa wood. Huang'e leaned on the zither and sang a clear song: "The sky is clear, the earth is vast and boundless, all things change and transform without limit. The sky is vast, the ocean is boundless, riding a raft gently floating beside the sun. Where did they reach, Qiong Sang? Knowing in their hearts that joy and harmony are endless."

People commonly refer to places of leisure and entertainment as "桑中" (Sangzhong). In the *Shijing* (Book of Songs), within the *Wei Feng* section, it is said: "Meet me at the mulberry grove." This is similar to the meaning. The son of the White Emperor responded with a song: "The four corners and eight directions are hard to reach, chasing light and shadows to the ends of the water. In the palace, at night, women weave under the moonlight. The tall, straight trees of the桐峰 (Tongfeng) are cut to make instruments like zithers and harps. The clear singing flows smoothly, the music is difficult to reach its peak, and the birds come to rest by the coastal waters." When Huang E gave birth to Shao Hao, he was also known as Qiong Sang (Poverty Mulberry), and also as Sang Qiu (Mulberry Hill). During the Warring States period, Sang Qiu wrote a book on *Yin-Yang* philosophy, which was a descendant of Shao Hao. Shao Hao was the ruler of the west, also known as Jin Tian (Metal Heaven), and also as Jin Qiong (Metal Poverty). At that time, there were five phoenixes, each with a color corresponding to a direction, gathering in the imperial court, and thus they were called Feng Niao (Phoenix Birds). Gold rang in the mountains, and silver flowed from the ground. Sometimes they resembled turtles and snakes, sometimes they appeared as human or ghostly forms. There were waters that twisted and turned, resembling the shapes of dragons and phoenixes, and mountains that wound and curved, resembling the form of a coiled dragon. Therefore, there were places named Longshan (Dragon Mountain), Guishan (Turtle Mountain), and Fengshui (Phoenix Water). These names also became surnames, with the surname Longqiu (Dragon Hill) appearing in Ban Gu's *Yi Wen Zhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature), and the surname Sheqiu (Snake Hill) appearing in *The Western Queen Mother's Divine and Strange Transmission*.

Emperor Zhuanxu, also known as the High Emperor of the Southern Sky, was the grandson of the Yellow Emperor and the son of Changyi. Changyi was by the riverbank when he encountered a black dragon carrying a dark jade map. At the time, an old man told Changyi, "Your child will follow the water element and become a king." Ten years later, Zhuanxu was born, and his hand had markings resembling a dragon, as well as a jade map. That night, Changyi looked up at the sky, and the North Star transformed into an old man. When Zhuanxu ascended the throne, all unusual signs of good fortune and blessings gathered, and even those who did not follow the correct calendar came from distant lands. The emperor bowed to the spirits of the four directions, and the feudal lords held jade disks as a sign of respect. All the feudal lords had their proper ranks and order. Those who received cultural virtues were given bells and chimes, while those who received martial virtues were given spears and halberds. There was a golden bell that floated and a clear jade chime that sank. When brushed with feathers, the sound could be heard for a hundred miles. A stone floated on water as light as duckweed or algae, and it could be used as a chime without further processing. When the emperor held court with the world's nations, and when the music of the refined arts was played, the sound was clear and harmonious, like the feathers falling from the clouds, and the whales and sharks surged through the calm sea. There was a sword that dragged its shadow and extended into the sky. If there was war in any direction, the sword would fly up and point to that direction, indicating victory. When not in use, the sword made sounds like a dragon or tiger in the scabbard.

To the north of the Sea of Mist, there is the country of Bodi. Its people are clothed in feathers, able to fly without wings, casting no shadow at noon, and living for a thousand years. They eat black river water algae and drink the fat of the yin mountain laurel. They ride the wind to soar and ride the waves to arrive. The warmth of the central land causes their feathered clothing to gradually fall off. The emperor then used patterned leopardskin as decoration. They presented black jade rings, colored like pure lacquer, and offered a thousand black horses. The emperor used them to drive iron wheels, exerting effort in distant and remote places. They relied on the wind and rode the black river to return to their country. To the north of the Dark River, there is a forest of purple laurel trees, whose fruit is like dates, eaten by the gods. Han Zhong, in his four-character poem on gathering medicine, said: "The laurel of the dark river, its fruit is large like dates. If one eats it, they will age after the heavens." Emperor Ku The consort of Emperor Ku was a daughter of the Zou and Tu clans. The Yellow Emperor drove away the evil of Chiyou, moving the good people to the land of Zou and Tu and the bad people to the northern place. Their ancestors named their clan after the land, and later divided into the Zou and Tu clans. She walked without touching the ground, always treading on wind and clouds, traveling in the rivers Yi and Luo. The emperor then invited her, taking her as his consort. The consort often dreamed of swallowing the sun, and then gave birth to a son. After eight dreams, she gave birth to eight sons. The world calls them "Eight Gods," also "Eight Bright Ones," "Eight Heroes," and "Eight Powers," describing their divine power and brilliance, they illuminate all things, and countless beings are influenced by their divine wisdom.

North of the Mingshai Sea lies the Kingdom of Bodi. Its people wear garments of feathers, yet they fly without wings, casting no shadow at noon, and live for a thousand years. They eat black river algae and drink the fragrant oil of the Yinshan mountain camphor. They soar upon the wind and arrive by riding the waves. When the air of the Central Kingdom grows warm, their feathered garments gradually fall away. The emperor then replaced them with striped leopards as adornment. They presented black jade rings, dark as pure lacquer, and offered a thousand black steeds. The emperor used them to drive iron-wheeled chariots, journeying to distant and remote lands. The people of Bodi travel upon the wind and across the Black River to return to their homeland. North of the Anhe River, there are groves of purple camphor trees, their fruit resembling dates, which the immortals consume. Han Zhong, in a four-character verse about gathering herbs, wrote: "The camphor of the Anhe River, its fruit as large as dates. Eat it and you will grow old after the heavens." Gaoxin The consort of Emperor Ku was a daughter of the Zou Tu clan. After the Yellow Emperor vanquished the cruel Chi You, he relocated the virtuous people to the land of Zou Tu and the wicked to the northern land of Youbei. Their ancestors were named after the land, and later the clan split into the Zou and Tu surnames. The daughter never set foot on the earth, but walked upon wind and clouds, wandering along the rivers Yi and Luo. The emperor met her and took her as his consort. She often dreamed of swallowing the sun, and thus bore a son; after eight such dreams, she bore eight sons. The world called them the "Eight Spirits," also known as the "Eight Yis," for "yi" means brightness; they were also called the "Eight Heroes" and the "Eight Powers," referring to their divine strength and wisdom. Their brilliance illuminated all things, and the countless multitudes were imbued with their divine insight.

There is a country called Danqiu, which presented a rhinoceros horn jar to be used for storing dew. The emperor's virtue permeates distant lands, and the jar is used to fill the kitchen with dew. Rhinoceros horn is a type of stone, and those from the south are considered superior. Nowadays, skilled horsemen can identify the color of a horse's brain by breaking it open after the horse's death. If the brain color is like blood, the horse can travel ten thousand miles a day and fly through the air; if the brain color is yellow, it can travel a thousand miles a day; if the brain color is green, its neigh can be heard for hundreds of miles; if the brain color is black, it can enter water without getting wet and travel five hundred miles a day; if the brain color is white, it has great strength and is fierce. Now, most vessels are made in red, and if made by humans, they often fail to be functional and are quite crude. The people of Danqiu can identify the brain color of a horse by listening to its neigh. The land of Danqiu is home to nightmarish creatures known as Yecha Juba, who can make bottles, bowls, and musical instruments from red rhinoceros horn. These items are all exquisite and delicate. If Chinese people use them, they cannot be harmed by demons. Another saying goes that rhinoceros horn is the blood of evil spirits, coagulated into this substance. In ancient times, Emperor Huangdi defeated Chiyou and the various evil spirits, filling rivers and valleys with blood and bones. After several years, the blood coagulated into stone, the bones turned white like ash, and the fat flowed into springs. Thus, the south has the Fatty Spring and the White Chalk Mountain, which look majestic and white like frost and snow. There is also Danqiu, which burns once every thousand years, and the Yellow River, which clears once every thousand years, considered great omens by the most virtuous rulers. The land of Danqiu is rich in ghost blood, which transforms into red stone, which is rhinoceros horn.

In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

Emperor Yao When Emperor Yao was on the throne, his virtuous rule illuminated the realm. By the banks of the Yellow and Luo rivers, a jade tablet a foot square was found, depicting the forms of heaven and earth. He also received the auspicious sign of a golden jade, with inscriptions clearly arranged, recording the beginning of the creation of heaven and earth. After the four malevolent beings were eradicated, good people came to serve, and officials were appointed to their positions, with the proper order established. Emperor Yao then appointed Yu the Great to clear the rivers and lakes. In the lands of Wu and the northern regions, there were no calamities or disasters. Among the creatures that fly and swim, they naturally became tame and harmonious. In the ruins of Youzhou and north of Yushan, there was a bird that could sing well, with a human face and a bird's beak, eight wings and one foot, feathers like a pheasant, and it did not touch the ground when walking. It is called the Qingli, and its sound is like bells, chimes, flutes, and pipes. The *Shi Yu* says, "When the Qingli sings, it is a time of peace." Therefore, in times of great prosperity and clarity, the Qingli would soar and sing in the marshes and lakes, its voice harmonizing with the music of the universe, flying but not moving. When Yu tamed the waters and earth, it settled in rivers and mountains. Wherever it gathered, there would surely be a sage born. Since ancient times, when casting various bronze vessels, they all depicted its form and inscribed praises, which have continued to the present day. After Emperor Yao had been on the throne for thirty years, a large raft floated in the Western Sea, and on it there was light, which shone at night and disappeared during the day. The people by the sea watched its light, which fluctuated in size, like the rising and setting of stars and the moon. The raft often floated around the four seas, completing a cycle every twelve years, and then began again. It is called the 'Guan Yue Cha' or 'Guaxing Cha,' and immortals lived on it.

The Immortals wash their mouths with dew, and the light of the sun and moon is as if it were dark. During the late periods of the Yu and Xia dynasties, this phenomenon was no longer recorded. Those who travel the sea still speak of it as a marvel. To the west of the Western Sea, there is a Floating Jade Mountain. At the foot of the mountain lies a great cave, within which is water that appears like fire. By day, it is dim and unclear, but at night it illuminates the outside of the cave. Even when waves and tides surge, its light does not fade; this is called "Yin Fire." During the reign of Emperor Yao, the light of the Yin Fire blazed forth, transforming into a red cloud that illuminated the rivers with its brilliant red glow. Those who travel the sea named it "Chenran," corresponding to the era of Fire Virtue. Emperor Yao ruled for seventy years, during which young phoenixes came annually, qilins roamed the marshes, and owls fled to the desolate wilderness. A country from the distant land of Zhi contributed the Double-Eyed Bird, also known as the "Chongming Bird," which is said to have two eyes. It resembles a chicken but sings like a phoenix. Sometimes it sheds its feathers and flies with its flesh, capable of chasing away fierce beasts and tigers, preventing evil disasters and malevolent forces from causing harm. It was fed with divine fat. Sometimes it comes once a year, and sometimes it does not appear for several years. The people of the country would sweep and clean their doors and windows, hoping for the arrival of the Chongming Bird. When it did not come, they would carve wood or cast metal to create images of the bird, placing them in their doors and windows, which would naturally cause the demons and evil spirits to retreat. Today, on the first day of the year, people either carve wood, cast metal, or paint images of chickens on their windows, as a legacy of this ancient practice.

Shun the Sage Shun the Sage reigned for ten years, during which five elderly sages roamed the capital. Shun honored them as teachers, and their words reached the beginning of creation. When Shun abdicated in favor of Yu, the five sages departed, and no one knew where they went. Shun then established a temple to the Five Planets to worship them. That night, five long stars appeared, a gentle breeze blew, and auspicious signs manifested in the form of connected pearls and a united jade disk. From all over the world, people came through multiple translations to pay homage. A distant country sent its people to court Shun, asking about signs of good and bad fortune. They replied: "In the past, beyond the North Pole, there was the vast sea of Tong, hidden high above the sun. There were a great fish and a large dragon, whose shapes were unknown. When they breathed, the eight directions were darkened, and when they flapped their fins, the five great mountains trembled. During the time of Yao, the great dragon caused harm by entangling itself in the sky, and when it did so, the three rivers overflowed, and the seas and rivers flowed together." The three rivers are the Milky Way, the Yellow River, and the Central River. These three rivers sometimes flow and sometimes block, but under the governance of a sage-king, the water is clear and pure, with no foam or turbulence.

When Emperor Shang Jun was in power, the world was in chaos, and tales of a giant fish swallowing the sun and a dragon coiling around the sky circulated, which were baseless rumors. This story claims that the sun was swallowed and stars rained down, which is somewhat true but not entirely accurate. Thus, it caused later generations to be confused, attributing it to groundless tales, especially for their broad appeal and strange nature, recording their vast strangeness and magnificent beauty. Shun was buried in the wilds of Cangwu, and there was a bird like a sparrow that came from Dan Prefecture. It emitted a five-colored mist, swirling like clouds, and was called the "Pengxiao Sparrow." It could fly in groups and carry soil to form mounds and tombs. This bird could change its shape and color, gathering on high trees. On trees, it was a bird; on the ground, it was an animal; its transformations were unpredictable. It often roamed the shores of Dan Sea and sometimes visited the wilds of Cangwu. It carried blue sand pearls and accumulated them into hills and mounds, which were called "Zhuqiu" (Pearl Mound). The pearls were light and fine, blown by the wind like dust, and were called "Zhuchen" (Pearl Dust). Nowadays, in the outskirts of Cangwu, mountain dwellers who gather herbs sometimes find blue stones, round and clean like pearls, which, if consumed, grant immortality, and those who wear them become lighter. Therefore, the immortal Fang Hui wrote in his seven-character ode on the Southern Mountain: "The pearl dust is round, clean, and light, and those who consume it gain eternal life."

Two thousand miles west of Ji Province lies a land renowned for filial piety. The people there live to be three hundred years old, weaving reed into their garments, as described in the *Book of Documents* as "island people wearing grass and leaf clothing." When they die, they are buried in the wilderness, where birds carry soil to build their mounds, and beasts dig their graves, with no markers or trees placed over them. For those who have passed away, the living carve wooden likenesses to honor them as if they were still alive. The inhabitants are brave and fierce, able to bite through metal and stone, with their tongues square at the tip and narrow at the base. They can lift a thousand catties with their hands, and when they draw on the ground with their claws, great springs flow forth. They are skilled at raising animals, venturing into the sea to catch dragons, which they raise in circular chambers for use in sacrifices. In ancient times, Huangdi (Yellow Emperor) defeated Chiyou, eliminating all evil, and singled out this place as a land of filial piety. All nations admired it, and thus Shun established it as a country of filial piety and courtesy. When Shun succeeded Yao, the people of this land brought jade and silk to pay homage, receiving special guest treatment, setting them apart from other barbarians. The customs also extend to birds, beasts, and insects, all in harmony with the balance of yin and yang. In a moment, the mountains turn once, the seas dry up, and fish and dragons live on land. A red bird like a Peng covers the dragons and fish with its wings. The dragon taps the sky with its tail to request rain, while the fish suck in the sun's light. When darkness falls, it is as if a solar eclipse has occurred, and stars and rain fall together. Shun then prayed to the spirits of the sea and mountains, and all nations called him a sage. The virtue he spread widely brought forth all auspicious signs.

In the kingdom of Nanxun, there is a cave with a hidden source that connects to the earth's veins. Within this cave live hairy dragons and hairy fish, which periodically shed their skin in the vast marshes. Fish and dragons coexist in the same cave. The kingdom presented a pair of hairy dragons, one male and one female, leading to the establishment of officials responsible for caring for the dragons. This practice continued uninterrupted through the Xia Dynasty, and the clan named itself after it. When Yu the Great tamed the floods, he rode one of these dragons. When the four seas were pacified, the dragon was released into the river bend.

Record states: According to the *Spring and Autumn Annals* commentary, "Stars fell like rain, yet the night remained bright." The *Huainanzi* says, "When the qilin fought, the sun and moon eclipsed; when the whale died, a comet appeared." The waxing and waning, eclipses, and other celestial changes are not clearly detailed in the sacred texts. Comets and other celestial anomalies are recorded as signs of calamity in historical records. The qilin fighting and the whale dying are not mentioned in previous scriptures. Seeking in the true edicts, it seems obscure. Record further states: After examining various books, from Fuxi to the Yellow Emperor, the Bird Clan, Emperor Ku, and the time of Yao and Shun, the practice of abdicating the throne to a worthy successor was passed down, and their symbols and names were recorded, but none were as great as Yao. According to the *Yi Jing* commentary, Yao was the embodiment of yang energy and virtue, aligning with the cosmic order. Examining ancient times, this is called the supreme sage. Heaven is the greatest, and Yao emulated it. The peaceful transfer of power to Shun, the harmonious succession, clearly represented the sun and moon. Their records are vast and long, their virtues and miracles distinct. Examining ancient legends and seeking consensus in the middle ages, the teachings varied, and virtues emerged in succession. Pointing out various explanations, it seems vague. The mysterious and elusive nature of spiritual phenomena is beyond the understanding of the wisest. How can we lightly dismiss their existence or non-existence? Liu Zizheng said, "All hearsay is less reliable than personal experience, and personal experience is less reliable than direct observation."

Why is this so? Divine transformations are sudden and unpredictable, appearing and disappearing without regularity, beyond the scope of superficial understanding or calculation, and beyond the reach of ordinary human thought and measurement. For example, unusual occurrences involving dragons, fire, birds, and water; mythical creatures such as phoenixes, qilins, and insects; ghostly and monstrous forms; sudden and fleeting appearances—all arise spontaneously from wind and clouds, transforming through gold and jade. These phenomena are not fully recorded in the bronze vessels of the Xia dynasty, nor are they reliably recorded in the *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas). The legend of the celestial boat, the story of the double-brightened moon and the fruit of the osmanthus tree, flames emerging from ice and wood, insects born in a fiery mountain, people with unusual physical characteristics, scenes of dragons shedding their bones and transforming into flesh—all arise and depend on wind and clouds, nurtured by rain and dew, already depicted in many images, now seen as remarkable in many records. Vast and far-reaching, dim and scattered writings, various schools of thought that are often abstract and impractical, each holds these differences in high regard, not confined to a single interpretation.

**Shiyiji, Volume 2** **Xia Yu** Emperor Yao appointed Xia Gun to control the floods, but after nine years, there was no success. Gun drowned himself in the Yu marsh and transformed into a black fish. Sometimes raising its whiskers and shaking its scales, it would leap across the long waves. Those who saw it called it the "river spirit." The Yu marsh is connected to the rivers and seas. The people by the sea built a temple to Gun in the Yu mountains and offered sacrifices throughout the four seasons. Often seen with black fish and dragons leaping out, spectators were amazed and fearful. When Emperor Shun appointed Yu to clear the rivers and stabilize the mountains, he crossed vast seas by using turtles as bridges and scaled green mountains by riding on divine dragons. He traveled across the realms of the sun and moon, except for not treading on the land of Yu, all due to the influence of Yu's virtue. The transformations of Gun are described differently in various accounts, but the transformations are similar, and the appearance differs. Black fish and yellow fish, the four sounds are confused, in written transmission, the text has flowed, the character for "Gun" sometimes has a "fish" radical and a "xuan" radical. Various doubts and interpretations, all are briefly recorded.

The Record states: The creation of written records began in the annals of the Yellow Emperor, when the Dao was simple and the customs were pure, and writing was valued for its substance. Descending to the era of Tang and Yu, and continuing through the Three Dynasties, the world's rituals and sacrifices have long been forgotten, and the records span a vast expanse of time. Successive wise rulers and scholars have been concerned about the decline of the Dao. Therefore, precious texts inscribed on jade tablets and gold ropes, and ancient scripts resembling insects and birds, have been hidden in remote mountains and forests, or concealed within the walls of houses; some have been lost in times of chaos and disorder, and the affairs of books and learning have been put aside. The records and traditions of the past have been scattered to foreign lands. Thus, the forms of characters have changed with the times, and the pronunciation and meaning of words vary by region. Through the periods of the Shang and Zhou dynasties, and again during the Qin and Han dynasties, bamboo slips and silk books were burned and torn, and ancient burial sites and texts were destroyed. Examining the remains of decayed texts and gathering hearsay and oral traditions, it is said that the character "己亥" was corrected in earlier doubts, and the character "三豕" was clarified in later errors. The *Zi Nian* chronicles, spanning a thousand years, are in agreement with the sages, and by analyzing texts to understand their underlying principles, this statement can be relied upon if it is true. The *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) states: "Emperor Yao exiled Gun to Yu Mountain." The *Spring and Autumn Annals* commentary says: "His spirit transformed into a yellow dragon and entered Yu Lake."

In the mountains, one transforms into a mythical creature, and upon entering the water, one changes into a fish. Animals rely on the mountains, and fish depend on the water, each changing according to its nature. Examining the canonical texts and consulting various sayings, whether true or similar, all are briefly recorded. Yu the Great cast the Nine Tripods, five of which corresponded to the yang principle, and four represented the yin number. He instructed the master craftsman to use female metal for the yin tripod and male metal for the yang tripod. The tripod always remained full, used to predict the auspiciousness or inauspiciousness of the weather. During the reign of King Jie of Xia, the water in the tripod suddenly boiled. By the end of the Zhou dynasty, all nine tripods trembled—these were all signs of impending doom. Later sages, following Yu's example, cast tripods generation after generation. Yu made great efforts to dig canals and divert rivers, leveling mountains. A yellow dragon trailed its tail in front, and a black turtle carried green mud behind. The black turtle is the messenger of the river spirit. The turtle's neck has a seal, the characters are all ancient seals, and the words are the names of the nine provinces and mountains and rivers. Wherever Yu excavated, he sealed it with green mud, and the black turtle stamped it. Today, people gather earth to mark boundaries, which is a remnant of this practice.

Yu carved the Dragon Gate mountain, also known as Longmen. He reached a vast cave, deep for dozens of miles, dark and impossible to continue. Yu then carried fire and proceeded. There was a beast like a pig, carrying a luminous pearl, its light like a candle. There was also a blue dog, barking ahead. Yu calculated it was about ten miles, but was confused by day and night. When he became aware and it started to brighten, he saw the pig and dog had transformed into human form, all wearing black clothes. He also saw a god, with a snake body and a human face. Yu then spoke with the god, who showed Yu the diagram of the Eight Trigrams, arranged on a golden tablet. There were also eight gods attending. Yu said, "Was the Holy Son born of Huaxu, are you?" The god replied, "Huaxu is the goddess of the Nine Rivers, who gave birth to me." He then took out a jade tablet and gave it to Yu, one foot two inches long, to correspond to the twelve hours, to measure the heavens and earth. Yu then held this tablet and used it to level the waters and earth. The god with the snake body was Fuxi, the legendary ruler.

The Records state: The traces of the divine are hard to seek, and the obscure are hard to discern. Between the vague and the dim, what is heard and seen is used to deceive. If we measure it by the principles of the unseen, the ancient texts provide some indication. Thus, Peng Sheng appeared to people in Bei Qi, and the King of Zhao showed his form as a black dog. All these events are recorded in the annals of Lu, serving as evidence for the states of Qi and Han. In remote antiquity, the events may differ, but the divine essence remains the same. The strange occurrences of holding pearls and spitting out candles, the spirits are equally remarkable. As for the vast traces of Yu, the dim origins of the divine, these are beyond the comprehension of the common people. From Fuxi to the Xia Dynasty's Yu, years have passed long and vast, generations have been numerous and far-reaching. Hence, they share the brilliance of the sun and moon, aligning with the yin and yang. Through countless generations and hundred kings, their feelings were different but their paths converged. Participating in the timing and path, seeing ten thousand years as morning and evening, compressing countless eons into an inch of time. Why lament the possibility of ghosts and spirits, and doubt the meeting of Fuxi and Yu? King Tang of Shang At the beginning of the Shang Dynasty, there was a divine woman named Jian Di, who wandered in the mulberry fields. She saw a black bird leave an egg on the ground, with five-colored patterns, forming the word 'eight hundred.' Jian Di picked it up, stored it in a jade box, and covered it with a red ribbon. At night, she dreamed of a divine mother, who said to her, "If you carry this egg, you will give birth to a holy child, to continue the golden virtue." Jian Di then carried the egg, and after one year, she became pregnant. After fourteen months, she gave birth to Qi. The legacy of eight hundred years, matching the text of the egg. Even though it faced drought and misfortune, the descendants prospered.

Fu Yue, leasing himself as a laborer, ground grain in a deep quarry to support himself. He dreamed of riding on clouds circling the sun and divined a favorable hexagram for establishing a feudal lord. After a year, Tang of Shang invited him with gifts of jade and silk to serve as his prime minister. King Zhou's moral corruption led him to intend to attack the feudal lords. He ordered Feilian and Erya to kill virtuous officials, seize their treasures, and bury them under the Qiongtai Terrace. He also ordered Feilian to deceive nearby states within the feudal system, causing continuous alarms. King Zhou ascended the terrace to see where the alarms were coming from and then sent troops to attack the country, killing the ruler, imprisoning the people, taking their women as concubines, and indulging in excessive cruelty. Gods and people were filled with anger. Red birds carrying fire appeared frequently, like stars illuminating the sky, confusing the alarm signals. King Zhou became confused and ordered the states to extinguish their alarms. The common people were relieved, and all the states became peaceful. When Wu attacked King Zhou, a woodsman and a shepherd found a jade seal in a high bird's nest, inscribed with the text: "The water element is about to decline, while the wood element is about to flourish." The text was written in large seal script, indicating that the Shang dynasty's reign had ended, and the virtue of the Zhou dynasty was rising. Therefore, two-thirds of the world's territories belonged to the Zhou. Thus, the common people lamented the late fall of the Shang and wished for the Zhou to come sooner.

Shiyang was a musician of the Shang Dynasty. Since the establishment of music, this role has been continuously followed through generations. When it came to Shiyang, he was proficient in explaining the principles of yin and yang and clearly understood celestial phenomena. His true nature was unfathomable. The lineage of musicians was distant and broken, with some appearing and others disappearing. During the time of the Yellow Emperor, he served as the official in charge of music. By the time of the Shang Dynasty, he comprehensively revised the music of the Three Sovereigns and Five Emperors. When he played a single-stringed zither, all earth deities ascended; when he blew the jade flute, all heavenly gods descended. During the time of the Yellow Emperor, he was already several hundred years old, listening to the music of various countries to discern the signs of prosperity and decline. At the end of the Xia Dynasty, he carried musical instruments to flee to the state of Yin (Shang Dynasty). King Zhou of Shang was indulgent in music and beauty, and he imprisoned Shiyang in a secret palace, intending to inflict the most severe punishment. Shiyang, once imprisoned, played the pure shang, flowing zheng, and cleansing jiao tones. The prison official reported this to King Zhou, who still found it unsatisfactory and said, "This is ancient and distant music, not something I can enjoy." Despite this, he did not release Shiyang. Shiyang then played more songs that bewitched the soul and enchanted the spirit, to entertain and prolong the night, thereby escaping the punishment of the hot iron pillar. When King Wu of Zhou rose to power, Shiyang crossed the Pu River and passed away; some say he died in the underwater realm. Therefore, people of the states of Jin and Wei carved stone and cast metal to create his image, and established worship that continues without interruption.

The *Records* state: The *Three Caves*, *Five Classics*, and various esoteric and astrological writings all claim that Jian Di (简狄) conceived the founder of the Shang dynasty, Xi (契), by swallowing a swallow's egg. The *Book of Songs* says: "By the decree of heaven, the black bird descended and gave birth to the Shang." This is the correct version of the text. This account speaks of conception through divine inspiration, with differing opinions among various sources, so it is recorded to highlight their distinctions. Fu Yue (傅说) left behind his labor as a commoner and was released from his position as a hired worker, responding to the omen and coming to serve as a minister, truly demonstrating his prescience. He is similar to Jiang Ziya (姜子牙) who returned to Zhou after fishing, yet different from Yi Yin (伊尹) who carried a tripod before becoming a prime minister. When the right time comes, even the most unlikely paths will lead to success. This is the clear teaching of past sages and the highest principle of learned individuals. "To accept one's fate with joy and understanding" is indeed a timeless truth. To achieve immortality in death is what we call fame. A bird may leave no reputation for its descendants, but its wings spread far and wide through the ages. Like precious metals and stones, dust cannot bury their steadfastness; like the clear rivers of Jing and Pu, the muddy waters of Zi and Wei cannot mix with their purity. Shiren (师延) faced the tyranny of King Zhou and adapted his steps to survive, using his authority to navigate and gain assistance, observing the times and serving his master, preserving his life and escaping. This is what is meant by being able to find a way out in adversity and ultimately escaping through wisdom. Therefore, his image is preserved in paintings and inscriptions on stone, valued for his harmonious and joyful contributions and esteemed for the far-reaching influence of his divine actions. As for the rewards given to Jixian (计然) for his wisdom, inscribed in gold to honor his virtue, they pale in comparison.

Zhou King Wu of Zhou launched an eastern campaign against King Zhou of Shang and crossed the river at night. At that time, the clouds were as bright as day, and eight hundred clans all sang together. There was a large bee resembling a red bird, which flew and landed on King Wu's boat. He then painted the image of the bird on his flag. On the next day, King Zhou was defeated and executed, and King Wu named his boat "Bee Boat." In the second year of Duke Ai of Lu, the people of Zheng attacked Zhao Jianzi and captured his bee flag, which was of the same type. [This event is recorded in the *Taigong Liutao* (Six Secret Teachings of Taigong).] King Wu ordered the image of the bee to be painted on his banners, considering it a good omen. Today, people's banners and letters all feature bird paintings, which are remnants of this tradition. Three years after King Cheng of Zhou ascended the throne, a delegation from the country of Nili came to pay homage. They said: Since leaving their country, they often traveled through the clouds, hearing the sound of thunder below; or entering hidden caves, they heard the sound of waves above. They determined the direction of their country by observing the sun and moon, and calculated the seasons by the cold and heat. When examining the calendar of their country, it matched the Chinese calendar. The king received them with the courtesy due to foreign guests.

### Final Translation In the fourth year, the country of Zhantu offered a young phoenix. It was transported in a jade chariot adorned with five-colored jade, driven by a red elephant, and arrived at the capital. The phoenix was raised in a sacred bird garden, fed with heavenly nectar and cloud fruit, both from the Upper Yuan realm. When the phoenix first arrived, its feathers were not yet brilliant; after King Cheng's enfeoffment of Mount Tai and the establishment of the Altar of the Earth, its feathers became splendid. All flying and running creatures in China ceased to make noise, all submitting to the arrival of the divine bird. When King Cheng died, the phoenix suddenly flew away. During the time when Confucius served as the governor of Lu, a divine phoenix appeared. By the end of Duke Ai's reign, the phoenix no longer appeared, hence the saying "The phoenix does not come," which is a cause for sorrow. In the fifth year, there was a country named Yinqi, nine thousand miles from the capital, which offered a skilled woman. She had a graceful and clean appearance, wearing finely woven and embroidered clothes with long sleeves and flowing skirts. When the wind came, she tied her sash to prevent herself from being blown away. This woman was skilled in weaving, using five-colored silk in her mouth and threading it with her hands to create patterned brocades. The people of the country offered various brocades: cloud-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds emerging from mountains; wall-patterned brocade, with designs resembling clouds over city walls; pearl-patterned brocade, with designs resembling pearls; seal script-patterned brocade, with designs resembling ancient Chinese characters; and lamp-patterned brocade, with designs resembling rows of lamps. Each piece was three feet wide. The men of the country diligently worked in farming and could till ten acres of land in a day. They also offered auspicious rice, with one stalk filling a cart. Thus, the contemporary four-character verse said, "Diligence in farming ten acres can bring forth excellent crops."

Sixth Year. The Kingdom of Ranqiu presented a pair of winged birds, one male and one female, enclosed in a jade cage. The envoys from the kingdom were all with sharp noses and wore clothing made of cloud and mist, as beautiful as the morning glow. They traveled through more than a hundred countries before finally reaching the capital. The journey through the mountains and rivers cannot be fully recorded. They crossed the Iron Pass, sailed across the boiling sea, and passed through Snake Island and Bee Mountain. The Iron Pass was steep and rugged, and the wheels of the vehicles were made of hard metal. By the time they reached the capital, the wheels were almost entirely worn down. The boiling sea was turbulent and boiling like a cauldron, with fish and turtles whose skin and bones were as hard as stone, suitable for making armor. When crossing the boiling sea, the bottom of the boat was lined with copper, preventing dragons from approaching. On Snake Island, they used a leopard skin roof and pushed the cart inside the house. On Bee Mountain, they burned hulusu wood, which, when burned, could kill all insects. The journey took more than fifty years, and they finally arrived at Luoyi. King Cheng performed a ritual at Mount Tai and offered sacrifices at the She Altar. He sent young children from their country, and upon arriving in the capital, their hair turned white. Upon returning to Ranqiu, their appearance returned to youthful vigor. The winged bird was strong, looking like a magpie, carrying red clay from the South Sea, and nesting in the dark wood of Kun岑. It appeared when encountering a sage, signifying the auspicious and extraordinary nature of Duke Zhou assisting a sage.

In the seventh year, in the southernmost part of the southern frontier, there was a country called Fulu. The people of this country were skilled in ingenious transformations, able to change their form and attire. When they were large, they could summon clouds and mists; when they were small, they could enter the tiniest spaces. They adorned themselves with gold, jade, feathers, and fur. They could spew clouds and fire, and when they roared, it sounded like thunder. They could transform into the shapes of rhinos, elephants, lions, dragons, snakes, dogs, and horses, or turn into tigers, buffaloes, and even give birth to humans from their mouths. They could perform all kinds of entertainments, twisting and turning in the palm of one's hand. Their human forms could be several inches or even a few centimeters long, full of supernatural and sudden changes, displaying their beauty and splendor. The Music Bureau had all transmitted this art, and even in later generations, people still learned it, though they might only capture the rough outline and lose the essence. Generation after generation, it never ceased, and therefore, it is commonly called the "Bao Hou" art. The name "Fulu" has been distorted and passed down to this day.

King Zhao had reigned for twenty years. One day, while seated in the Bright Hall during the day, he took a light nap. Suddenly, he dreamed of thick, lush clouds rising, and a person appeared with clothing made entirely of feathers. This person was named "Feathered One." In the dream, the Feathered One spoke to King Zhao, asking about the ways of achieving immortality. The Feathered One said, "Your Majesty's wisdom and spirit have not yet been fully cultivated. If you wish to attain eternal life, it cannot be achieved." King Zhao knelt and asked for teachings on abstaining from desires. The Feathered One then pointed to King Zhao's heart, and to his shock, it split open. King Zhao woke up with blood soaking his robe and bedding, and he fell ill with heart trouble. He immediately stopped eating and withdrew from all pleasures. After ten days, the Feathered One appeared again in his dream and said, "I wished to replace your heart." The Feathered One then produced a small green bag, containing pills to restore the pulse and powders to replenish the blood. With a gentle touch, King Zhao's heart was healed. King Zhao asked for these remedies, which were stored in a jade vase and sealed with a golden cord. The Feathered One explained that if the remedies were applied to the feet, one could travel across thousands of miles of the world as if they were nearby. However, those who used them would eventually die after a certain period.

In the twenty-fourth year, the Tu Xiu Kingdom presented a pair of blue phoenixes and a pair of red magpies, each consisting of one male and one female. During the first month of summer, both the phoenixes and magpies molted and changed their plumage. The wings of the magpies were gathered to make fans, and the feathers of the phoenixes were woven to adorn the car canopies. The fans were known by four names: "Yoyou," "Tiaohé," "Kuiguāng," and "Zèyǐng." At that time, the kingdom of Dongou presented two women, one named Yanjuan and the other named Yanwu. The king had these two women take turns waving the fans, standing by his side, and the gentle breeze spread, cooling him pleasantly. These two women were eloquent and skilled in beautiful speech, adept at singing and laughing, and their footsteps left no trace on the ground, and their shadows were barely visible in the sunlight. When King Zhao drowned in the Han River, the two women accompanied him on the boat, holding him close, and they all drowned together. Therefore, the people of Jianghan still think of them today, and a shrine was established by the riverside. For several decades, people on the banks of the Jiang and Han rivers still saw King Zhao and the two women playing on a boat by the water. On the third day of the third month in the lunar calendar, during the ritual gathering at the shrine, some offered fresh and sweet delicacies, wrapped in fragrant herbs, and sank them in the water. Others used colorful silk pouches to hold the food, or metal and iron vessels, and sank them in the water to scare away dragons and water creatures, making them fear and not touch the food. The shrine by the water was called the Shrine of Invoking the Spirits. The feathers of the blue phoenix were woven into two coats, known as "Yùzhì" and "Xuānjī." Wearing these coats could ward off the cold. When King Li was exiled to Zhi, the people of Zhi found these coats strange, and they divided them, spreading them throughout the land of Zhi. Those who committed crimes punishable by death could redeem their lives by pulling out a single hair from the coat, which was worth ten thousand gold.

Record states: King Wu, endowed with divine wisdom, achieved victory and observed the mandate of heaven to carry out punishment. He did not mobilize a fierce army or engage in multiple battles; with one campaign, he established the royal cause, relying on divine power to harmonize auspicious signs. When it came to King Cheng, he established rituals and elevated music, and the virtue of the House of Ji was flourishing. He established Luoyang and housed the Nine Tripods, abolished capital punishment in the temple, and attracted the nations. Even the grand achievements of the great Yu in the flourishing summer and the rise of the Shang dynasty under Emperor Yi were not sufficient to match the achievements of King Wu and King Cheng. Thus, they could continue the legacy of Houji, follow the great virtue of Gong Liu, and the legacy of Wen and Wu did not decline, hence the *Da Ya* (Great Odes) called them "virtuous." They disseminated the sound and teachings beyond the eight corners of the world and spread benevolence and kindness beyond the nine regions. The domain of divine wisdom and governance, the far and near came to submit, none failed to cross mountains and seas, offering tribute on the distant and perilous roads.

Rare and extraordinary treasures filled the royal court; divine birds and mythical beasts roamed and gathered in the forests. Strange and beautiful objects, carved and shaped differently from human craftsmanship, were not recorded in any books but were not entirely absent from inscriptions and writings. When the royal envoy, traveling swiftly, reached beyond the borders of the sun and moon, at the boundary of darkness and light, he observed the stars to navigate and relied on the clouds and waves to travel far. This is what is meant by the path connecting the subtle and profound, and virtue permeating the dark and obscure. From the reigns of King Cheng and King Kang of Zhou onward, the world's sacrifices and offerings declined. King Zhao could not expand his distant legacy, establish a reputation and influence, and traveled south to Jing and Chu, departing from the proper duty of inspecting the regions, becoming entranced by the spirits of the Yangtze and Han rivers, and indulging excessively in pleasure. At the water's edge, he was summoned to ask, and the *Spring and Autumn Annals* deeply criticized this. Alas, the two consorts who died in sacrifice, and the three loyal retainers who remained steadfast. Their sincere devotion made them regard death as if it were life. To conform to the righteous path, it was better to strongly advise. The people of Chu pitied them, and they lost their lives in vain.

**The Book of Lost Tales, Volume III** **King Mu of Zhou** In the thirty-second year of King Mu's reign, he toured the entire world. He drove a golden and jade chariot, riding the wind and mist, rising from the mountain of the rising sun, traveling from dawn to dusk, reaching the boundary of Yu's county. He had ten scribes to record the places he traveled to. Additionally, he had ten chariots of jade flowers following him, to carry his books. The king drove eight divine horses: one was named "绝地" (Unearthly), whose hooves did not touch the ground; the second was named "翻羽" (Sky Soarer), which could fly faster than birds; the third was named "奔霄" (Sky Runner), which could travel a thousand miles at night; the fourth was named "超影" (Shadow Surpasser), which could follow the sun; the fifth was named "逾辉" (Radiant), whose coat was brilliantly shining; the sixth was named "超光" (Light Surpasser), which could create ten shadows with one form; the seventh was named "腾雾" (Mist Rider), which could rise through the mist; the eighth was named "挟翼" (Winged), which had physical wings. They took turns driving them, and he gently controlled the reins and moved slowly, encircling the entire domain of heaven and earth. The king had divine wisdom and far-reaching plans, making the tracks of the wheels spread across the four seas, thus extraordinary things came to him without being expected.

The Record states: All life arises from qi, rarely does it not distinguish itself through form. When it comes to comparing virtues and matching deeds, dragons and horses are of the same kind. Therefore, Cai Tan observed its intelligence, and Ji Wei assessed its talent. It is also manifest in the celestial diagrams and auguries, and recorded in the precious scrolls. It displays the auspicious signs of the sovereigns, harmonizes with the auspicious signs of the Yellow River and Luo River. Hence, its form is depicted in paintings, and its image is transmitted in bronze and jade. As for the legendary horses like Lu'er, Hualiu, Chijì, and Baidi, and the unique horses like Huangqu, Shanzi, and Yulun, they are incomparable. Thus, it can travel far to Jieshi and surpass the moving shadow, push open the celestial gates and surpass Gu and Xu. Without its destined path and pacing the sun, how could it be so! Gazing at the Crimson Palace and raising its head, pointing to the Jade Terrace and resting for a moment, only then can it match its shadow. As for the *Book of Poetry* and *Book of Documents*, there are many names and types. The red and black horses are beautiful in the open fields, and their bright forms shine in the empty valleys.

Or there are horses with a distinctive purple hue, clothed in blue and black, whose appearance is difficult to fully describe. Those with dragon-like patterns, such as the *yao* and *niao* breeds, are chosen for their swift and graceful movements, while others like the *qilin*, *luo*, and *jue* breeds are also esteemed for their speed and agility. None of these horses come to light without a bright and enlightened era, making them the divine treasures of successive dynasties. There are also horses such as the *bao*, *nie*, *yu*, and *li* breeds, some of which were famous in ancient Han times, others precious and born in Jibei, adorned by the imperial court and lined up in the imperial stables. They serve in the imperial carriage and pull the imperial carriage, but when they are abandoned in the lower stables, they long for service in the imperial stables, waiting for the recognition of Wu Ban and Qin Gong. They look up to the Heavenly Gate and feel increasingly distant, and gaze at the path to the clouds, finding it difficult to attain. If only Han Ai and Sun Yang could once again hold the reins, would they not be harmed by worn bits and broken reins, hidden and not advancing? Without divine insight into the distant and unknown, and a body that illuminates the dark, driving and riding among the dragons, exploring the heavens, searching thoroughly through ancient times, finding no equal.

In the thirty-sixth year, the king toured the eastern part of the Great Riding Valley. He pointed to the Palace of Spring Night, where he gathered the essential techniques of various alchemists and immortals. Chimerae, cranes, dragons, snakes, and other strange creatures emerged from nowhere. As night was approaching, the king set up a lamp for eternal life to illuminate himself, also known as Everlasting Radiance. He also lit jade fat candles throughout the palace and had phoenix brain lamps. There were also ice lotus flowers, which come from the ice valleys. These flowers were used to cover the cooking area, about seven or eight feet high, to prevent the light from spreading too far. The Queen Mother of the West arrived riding a green phoenix carriage, preceded by tigers and leopards with patterns, and followed by carved unicorns and purple deer. She wore red jade shoes, sat on a green rush mat, and had a yellow rush cushion, and they all gathered in a high meeting under the jade tent. She offered clear and pure jade-like oil as wine. She also offered deep-sea red flowers, sweet snow from Qinqiu, white lotuses from Kunliu, black jujubes from Yinji, thousand-year-old ice peaches, ten-thousand-year-old green lotus roots, and white oranges with blue flowers. White lotuses, which have a hundred seeds in one pod and thrive even in winter. Black jujubes, whose trees are a hundred fathoms tall, with fruits two feet long, small and soft seeds, and mature once every hundred years.

To the east of the mythical tree of Fuyu, five thousand miles away, lies the mountain of Pangran. On this mountain stands a peach tree with a circumference of a hundred zhang, its flowers a deep blue-black, and it bears fruit once every ten thousand years. The river of Yuyu flows to the east of Pangran Mountain, its waters forming small streams beneath a large pond, known as "Chenliu" or "Zhongquan." This river produces green lotus roots, each measuring a thousand zhang in length, with seven feet being the standard length. The mountain of Tiaoyang yields a mystical plant called Shenpeng, resembling wild thyme and growing to a height of ten zhang. During the early Zhou Dynasty, the people of the kingdom presented this plant to the Zhou court, which used it to construct the columns of the "Hao Palace." Within this palace were white oranges, whose flowers were a greenish-blue and whose fruits were white and as large as melons, fragrant for several miles. Harmonious music was played, and precious objects were arranged to reach the clouds. Among these objects were pipes made from the wood of Mount Cenhu, bells carved from the metal of the place Mufu, a quiet zither made from the wood of Mount Yuan, and light and resonant stone chimes made from the stone of the island Fuying. As the music was played and songs were sung, all spirits gathered. The heavens, known as Jun Tian, represent harmony and breadth. [Source: "The Travels of King Mu of Zhou."] Mount Cenhu is located in the western sea, with bamboo-like plants that, when cut into pipes and blown, produce the sound of a flock of phoenixes. The place Mufu produces fine copper, suitable for making bells and gongs. Mount Yuan has a circular shape, with a vast forest that remains undisturbed even in the strongest winds, its wood used to make zithers and harps, hence the name "Jingsese." Fuying, also known as the island of Yingshou, has green stones that can be used to make chimes. These chimes, measuring one zhang in length, are as light as goose feathers and produce sound due to their lightness. After the Queen Mother of the West and King Mu of Zhou finished their joyful singing, they commanded their carriage to rise into the clouds and depart.

**Duke Xi of Lu** In the fourteenth year of Duke Xi's reign, Duke Wen of Jin burned the forest to find Jie Zhitui. A white crow circled the smoke and made noise, and some of them gathered around Jie Zhitui, and the fire did not burn him. The people of Jin praised this event, and they built a high platform and named it the "Thinking about Jie Zhitui Platform." They planted the "Ren Shou" tree, which is similar to a pine but has long, soft branches and edible flowers. Hence, the *Lüshi Chunqiu* (Master Lü's Spring and Autumn Annals) states: "Among beautiful trees, there is the 'Ren Shou' tree." This is indeed the case. The country decreed that for hundreds of miles around the burned mountain, residents were not allowed to set traps, and it was called the "Benevolent Crow." Commonly, people also refer to white-breasted crows as "Benevolent Crows," and they are of the same kind. The record states: The Lord of Chu, Zi Ge, once said, "In ancient times, King Mu of Zhou wished to travel extensively and have his chariot tracks and horse prints seen everywhere." By consulting the *Zhu Shu* (bamboo slips), the *Shi Shi* (stone chambers), and the *Jin Shu* (golden ropes), we find that the *Shan Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas), *Er Ya* (Dictionary of Terms), and the *Da Zhuan* (Great Transmission) all agree on this matter, despite the passage of time. Famous mountains and great rivers, reaching their highest points, different lands and customs, none of which do not bow and kowtow. Eastward, one ascends the platform of the giants; westward, one feasts in the hall of the Queen Mother of the West; southward, one crosses the bridge made of giant turtles; northward, one travels through the land of accumulated feathers. Drinking wine by the Jade Pool and composing poetry, playing games by the well, inscribing stones at the Hill of Huangxuan, leaving no trace on the Heavenly Garden. Since the beginning of time, no such divine and extraordinary events have been recorded in historical texts.

**Duke Xī of Lu** In the fourteenth year of Duke Xī’s reign, Duke Wén of Jin burned the forest in search of Jiè Zhītuī. A white crow circled the smoke and cried out, sometimes perching beside Jiè Zhītuī, and the fire could not reach him. The people of Jin admired this, and they erected a high platform, naming it the "Thoughtful Smoke Hill." They planted the *Rénshòu* tree, which resembles the cypress but has long, supple branches and flowers that are edible. Thus, the *Lü’s Spring and Autumn* says: "Among beautiful trees, there is the blossom of the *Rénshòu*." This is precisely that tree. The state decreed that within hundreds of li of the burned mountain, the people were forbidden to set traps or nets, and this bird was called the "Benevolent Crow." Commonly, people also refer to crows with white chests as "Kind Crows," and these are of the same kind. Recorded thus: The Prime Minister of Chu, Zǐ Gé, once said: "In ancient times, King Mù wished to extend his heart across all lands, so that all under heaven would have the marks of chariots and horses." Examining the worm-eaten bamboo slips of the *Zhu Shū*, searching in the stone chambers, the records are unbroken like golden threads. The *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, the *Er Ya*, and the *Great Transmission*, though spanning many generations, all agree in their accounts. Famous mountains and great rivers, the highest peaks reached, distant lands with different customs—all without exception bowed deeply in reverence. Ascending the giant platform in the east, feasting in the Hall of the Queen Mother of Heaven in the west, crossing the bridge of turtles and crocodiles in the south, passing through the land of accumulated feathers in the north. Drinking from the Jade Pool and composing poetry, playing and wandering at Jingbo. Carving inscriptions on the Hill of Xuanyuan, leaving no trace upon the heights of Xuanpu. Since the beginning of the world, no account in the records has ever described such wondrous and miraculous events as these.

King Ling of Zhou In the twenty-first year of King Ling of Zhou's reign, Confucius was born during the reign of Duke Xiang of Lu. At night, two blue dragons descended from the sky and came to the room of Zhengzai, Confucius' mother. Through this dream, Confucius was born. Two divine maidens carried fragrant dew from the sky to bathe Zhengzai. The Heavenly Emperor played the music of the Central Heaven, arranged in the room of the Yan family. There was a voice in the air saying that the heavens had given birth to a sage child, so they sent down the harmonious sounds of bells and drums, which are different from the music of the common world. There were also five old men standing in Zhengzai's courtyard, who were the spirits of the five planets. Before Confucius was born, a qilin (a mythical creature) spat out a jade book in a house in Qufu. The text read: "The son of the water spirit, bound to the declining Zhou and the simple king." Thus, the two dragons circled the room, and the five stars descended to the courtyard. Zhengzai, being wise and discerning, recognized these as supernatural events. She tied a silk ribbon to the qilin's horn, and after two days, the qilin left. Diviners said: "Confucius is the descendant of King Tang of Shang, with the water element and the simple king's nature." By the end of King Jing of Zhou's reign, in the twenty-fourth year of Duke Ding of Lu, a man from Lu named Chu found a qilin in a large marsh and showed it to Confucius. The silk ribbon tied to the qilin's horn was still there. Confucius knew that his life was coming to an end, so he embraced the qilin, untied the ribbon, and wept profusely. Moreover, the time when the qilin appeared and the year when the ribbon was untied were nearly a hundred years apart.

The Record states: Carefully examining past histories and thoroughly reviewing ancient decrees, the theories of "Yuan Shen" and "Gou Ming," as well as the "Six Canons of Heaven and Earth," when studied for their general principles, align with what is recorded today. Discussing hidden mysteries, they are deeply influential. Therefore, all writers follow the precedents of ancient strategies, as the extensive virtues of the most sage are vast. Thus, revering virtue and honoring the Way, one must strive to fully realize their true essence. The Kunlun Mountains and the deepest seas cannot match its height or breadth. All living beings with consciousness look up to it as they would to the sun and moon. The Master was born during the late Zhou period, when the royal government gradually became deficient, lamenting the great Dao's imminent downfall and regretting the decline of refined culture. Thus, he sought out old regulations and established the Five Rituals, collected lost sounds and rectified the Six Musics, supporting the people like a pillar supports a house and serving as a vessel to navigate through countless generations. What is meant by honoring virtue and expanding one's career? Is this what it refers to? Mencius said, "A sage appears once every thousand years, called a continuous step." Since the cessation of writing, counted in years and generations, it is difficult to measure or estimate. Therefore, the words of learned individuals believe a new sage is coming, and subsequent doubts will be further clarified.

In the twenty-third year, the "Kunzhao" Terrace was built, also known as the "Xuanzhao" Terrace. Gathering rare woods and divine craftsmanship from all over the world, they obtained trees that grew in the shade of the Egu Valley.
