These trees were a thousand *shun* (a unit of length) tall, with intertwined grain and patterns, and with this one tree, the materials for the terrace were sufficient. The main trunk was used for beams and rafters, and the small branches for purlins and rafters. The wood had the shapes of dragons, snakes, and a hundred beasts. They also sifted water essence to make mud. The terrace was a hundred *zhang* (a unit of length) high, and it was ascended to look at the color of the clouds. At that time, there was Chang Hong, who could summon divine and strange phenomena. The king ascended the terrace and looked at the dense cloud formation. Suddenly, he saw two people riding on the clouds, with yellow beards and hair, not of the common folk. They rode on a carriage shaped like a flying dragon and phoenix, driven by a green *chi* (a mythical beast). Their clothes were all sewn from feathers. The king immediately welcomed them to the upper seat. At that time, the whole country was suffering from a great drought, with the ground cracking and trees burning. One of them first sang: "Can you make snow and frost?"

A breath was exhaled, and clouds rose, snow flew, and all those present were filled with awe. The ponds and wells in the palace froze solid, hard enough to be carved. Then, luxurious fur coats made from the armpits of foxes and rugs adorned with purple bear fur, which were gifts from the Western Regions, were placed on the platform, warming all who sat upon them. Another person sang, "I can make the seat become warm." With a flick of his finger on the seat, a warm breeze entered the room, and the fur coats and rugs were all discarded at the foot of the platform. At that time, Rongchengzi advised, "Great King, you regard the world as your home, but you employ unusual techniques, changing summer to winter and deceiving the people. This is not something the virtuous rulers Wen, Wu, and Zhou Gong would approve of." The king then distanced himself from Chang Hong and sought upright advisors. At that time, tributes were offered from different regions, including jade people and a stone mirror, which was white like the moon and reflected faces like snow, called the "Moon Mirror." There was also a jade person whose body could automatically rotate, and Chang Hong told the king, "It is attracted by your virtue." Therefore, the Zhou people killed Chang Hong for flattering him, and his blood turned into stone, possibly turning into jade, and his body was never found. There was a man named Hanfang who came from the land of Quxu. He offered a jade camel five feet tall, a phoenix made of amber six feet tall, and a fire crystal mirror three feet wide, which allowed one to see objects in the dark as if it were daylight. When speaking to the mirror, the reflection in the mirror responded with the same voice. Hanfang was a tall man, a hundred feet tall, with long hair reaching his knees, and his hands were painted with cinnabar in the shape of waxing and waning moon and sun, illuminating over a hundred steps. The Zhou people saw him and regarded him as a deity. During the later years of King Ling, his whereabouts were also unknown.

The text states: "When one is enticed by what is desirable, true virtue is lost; when one is confused by what is heard and seen, the will is changed: is this not referring to King Li of Zhou! He was controlled by luxury, played with divine powers in chaos, and the true teachings were swayed, leading to a thinning of moral standards. Why is that so? By indulging in the ways of immortals, he abandoned the teachings of Confucianism, and thus, the spirits of foreign lands were invoked, which have been revered for thousands of generations. To the extent that their influence spread far and wide, affecting the customs of distant peoples, these practices were not subject to the norms of the central authority or the seasons, and thus, spirits and divine beings from all directions gathered, and strange energies came from afar, spanning the heavens and the earth, reversing the five constants and shifting the four seasons, in the realm of the ineffable and the obscure, it is difficult to describe. Exploring the deepest mysteries and the limits of wisdom, how great it is! In serving one's lord, one should observe all rites, and loyalty is a great virtue. If there is a deviation, one should advise fully; if not followed, one should withdraw oneself. Thus, one can even sacrifice one's life without hesitation, pushing open doors and wheels, knowing death but not retreating. Like hands and feet protecting the head and eyes, like a boat crossing a great river, the bond between ruler and minister is paramount. However, he violated the principle of 'speaking frankly even if it offends,' acting to gain favor, and ultimately faced execution by beheading. How tragic! 容成 and 巽弘 cannot be mentioned together."

Shikuang, a legendary blind musician and scholar, is said to have lived during the reign of Duke Ling of Jin. He served as the head of the music office, with a profound understanding of music and rhythm, and composed a hundred military treatises. People of his time did not know his origins, and his history is obscure and hard to verify. During the reign of Duke Ping of Jin, he became famous for his knowledge of the yin-yang theory. He blinded himself to avoid distractions and devoted himself to the study of astronomy, mathematics, and music. He tested the pitch of bells and chimes to determine the seasons, with no deviation, not even a hair's breadth. The *Spring and Autumn Annals* do not record the specific era of Shikuang. Realizing his fate and nearing the end of his life, he wrote the *Baofu* (Treasure Symbol) in a hundred volumes. During the Warring States period of the Jin state, his books were lost or destroyed. Laozi, another name for the founder of Daoism, lived at the end of the Zhou dynasty and resided on the mythical mountain of Fanjing Dayushi, out of touch with the world. There were five elderly men with white hair, some riding cranes, some wearing feathers, with ears protruding from the top of their heads, round pupils, faces as white as jade, and holding green bamboo staffs. They engaged in discussions with Laozi about the principles of the universe. When Laozi retired to become a historian, seeking the way to submit to the Dao, scholars from all over the world competed to come to him. The five elders are the spirits of the five directions in Chinese cosmology.

In the land of Futi, two individuals with supernatural abilities were presented, who could alternate between old and young, appear as a shadow when invisible, and vanish upon hearing a sound. They produced a golden pot four inches long from their elbows, which had a seal of five dragons on top and was sealed with green clay. Inside the pot was black ink, thick like lacquer, which, when sprinkled on the ground or stone, formed characters of seal, clerical, and oracle bone script. This ink recorded the beginning of creation and human relationships and assisted Laozi in writing the *Tao Te Ching*, which comprised ten thousand words. The text was written on jade tablets, bound with golden ropes, and stored in a jade box. They worked day and night with great effort, their bodies tired and spirits weary. When the ink in the golden pot ran out, the two individuals cut out their hearts and drained their blood to replace the ink. They took turns drilling their skull bones to extract marrow, which was used as a substitute for oil and candles. When their marrow and blood were exhausted, they reached into their chests and found a jade tube containing powdered elixir, which they used to coat their bodies, and their bones returned to normal. Laozi said, "Further simplify and remove the complexities, leaving only five thousand words." When the work was completed, the two individuals disappeared without a trace. The record states: Zhuangzi said, "A virtue that matches heaven and earth still requires profound words." Observing Laozi, he held humility and gentleness as essential, drawing from emptiness and silence to return to the true. Knowing that the great simplicity had already been lost, he revealed profound writings to the world. Who can discern its voidness and explore its deep silence? Therefore, Confucius criticized his virtue, attributing it to divine inspiration, using the two individuals as an extreme metaphor, likening them to dragons. Shikuang set up thousands of intervals, ultimately concluding at the end of the Spring and Autumn period. The *Baopu Zi* called them the "sages of musical knowledge." Even the wonders of Rongcheng, the calendar of Dainao, the music theory of Kui and Xiang, and the hearing of Yanzhou could not surpass them.

Master Shijuan lived during the reign of Duke Ling of Wei, where he was skilled in composing music for various dynasties and adept at creating new melodies to replace ancient tunes. Thus, he composed music for the four seasons: in spring, songs evoking migrating geese and lotus flowers; in summer, tunes inspired by bright mornings, hot springs, and flowing gold; in autumn, melodies reflecting the autumn winds, white clouds, falling leaves, and blowing grass; and in winter, pieces that convey the imagery of frozen rivers, overcast skies, and heavy clouds. These four seasonal compositions were performed for Duke Ling. However, Duke Ling became deeply engrossed and confused by the music, neglecting state affairs. Qu Boyu approached the throne to advise, saying, "Although these compositions may promote the spirit of the music, they ultimately lead to excessive indulgence and are not in harmony with the *Feng* and *Ya* (the classic poetic styles). It is not fitting for me to recommend such music to Your Majesty." Duke Ling then abandoned the music and focused on state governance, and the people of Wei praised his transformation. Master Shijuan regretted his deviation from the *Ya* and *Song* (the classic musical styles) and his failure to adhere to the proper conduct of a minister. He withdrew from public life and went into seclusion. Qu Boyu burned the instruments at the Nine-Crossroads, fearing that they would be passed down and misused in future generations. The record states: Governing a state with honesty and straightforwardness as the priority, and leading the government with humility and frugality as the foundation. Therefore, the three types of bad influences and ten kinds of bad habits are mentioned in the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents) to emphasize the importance of avoiding them. The *Tangfeng* (Odes of Tang) values diligence and avoiding idleness. Duke Ling disregarded the clear warnings of the poets, indulging in luxury and confusion. Although he later regretted his initial mistake and changed his attitude after receiving advice, it is like an eclipse of the sun and moon, which does not fundamentally affect their brightness. Qu Boyu's intention was to guide the ruler, holding firm to integrity and sincerity. Master Shijuan understood the principles of advancing and retreating, observing faults to reveal benevolence. One ruler and two virtuous ministers, this is worthy of praise.

During the reign of Duke Jing of Song, there was an expert in star lore who was offered the position of a high-ranking official and lived in a high, multi-story building with extended wings to observe the signs in the sky. He was provided with luxurious food and valuable clothing. His meals included wild ducks from the Qi River, cooked with the essence of cinnamon; sparrows from the courtyard, steamed with honey foam; eels from the Qi and Zhang rivers, dried and preserved with green pepper; rice from the nine rivers, cooked with fragrant herbs; and clear, clean water from a well, used for washing. The water was washed with fine silk. The cook struck the bell according to the time and struck the stone chime when it was time to eat, indicating that the bell and chime were rung for every meal. Clothes for each season were hung, decorated with gold and jade in spring and summer, and warmed with jade in autumn and winter. Exotic incense was burned on the platform. Suddenly, a rustic person appeared, wearing rough clothing and carrying a bag, and knocked on the door to enter. He said, "I have heard that the king loves the art of Yin and Yang and the mysteries of celestial phenomena. I request to be seen." Duke Jing invited him to a high hall. He spoke of future omens and then spoke of past events without error. At night, he observed the stars and signs, and during the day, he calculated and examined charts. He did not wear valuable clothing or eat exotic food. Duke Jing thanked him, saying, "Now that the state of Song is in turmoil, without your help, how can I assist it?" The expert replied, "The distribution of virtue is uneven, and disorder will soon follow. Cultivate virtue to attract people, and heaven will respond with good omens, and people will admire the transformation." Duke Jing said, "Good." He then bestowed the surname 'Zi' and named him Wei, who is known as Zi Wei.

The Record states: Cóngzǐ, who served as the official in charge of astronomy for generations, excelled in observing the stars and their configurations, and was also like Zǐ Shèn and Bì Zào. King Jǐng treated him as if he were a deity, honored him with the highest courtesy, dressed him in clothes of unparalleled quality, and fed him with exotic delicacies. Even if it is said to be the finest of all dishes, compared to this, it seems humble. The *Spring and Autumn Annals* granted surnames based on one's birth or status and became famous due to their deeds, naming them after their role in observing the stars. By the end of the Warring States period, they wrote books on yin-yang theory. [From Bāngù's *Records of the Arts and Literature*.]

Yue plotted to conquer Wu, collecting rare treasures, beautiful women, and exotic delicacies to present to Wu. They sacrificed three animals to pray to heaven and earth, and sacrificed dragons and snakes to worship rivers and mountains. They pretended to mobilize millions of households from southern China to serve as laborers for Wu. Yue also had two beautiful women, one named Yiguang and the other named Xiuming, [who were also known as Xi Shi and Zheng Dan.] They were presented to Wu. Wu housed them in a room fragrant with pepper and adorned with fine pearl-hung curtains, lowering them in the morning to block the sun and raising them at night to welcome the moon. The two women sat together in front of the window, tidying their mirrors and applying makeup within the pearl-hung curtains. Those who secretly peeped at them were all moved and terrified, calling them divine beings. The King of Wu was bewitched and neglected his duties. When Yue's troops entered the country, he took the two women and fled to the Wu palace. As Yue's army entered chaotically, they saw the two women under a tree and all called them divine women, gazing at them but daring not to approach. Now inside the snake gate of the Wu city, there is a decaying tree stump that is still a place of worship for the divine women. At first, when the Yue king entered Wu, there were red birds flanking him and flying. When King Goujian became a hegemon, he built the Lookout for the Red Birds, saying that the red birds were unusual. Fan Li served as chancellor of Yue, bringing in thousands of gold each day. There were ten thousand household servants skilled in arithmetic. They collected rare goods from all over the world, accumulating them in the Yue capital to make them into objects. Copper and iron were piled up like mountains, sometimes hidden in wells and trenches, called 'Treasure Wells'. Beautiful faces and colors overflowed the inner chambers, called 'Wandering Palaces'. Since ancient times, there has never been anything like this.

The Record states: The *Yijing* (Book of Changes) extols humility and benefit, while the *Shujing* (Book of Documents) emphasizes wise counsel; thus, the conduct of ministers should be guided by these principles. It is said, "To know nothing one should not do is loyalty." Fan Li expounded the fundamentals of statecraft, and King Goujian of Yue rose to dominance, eventually becoming the ruler of the states of the south, known for his wealth and strength, which were the result of his abilities. Therefore, he was able to feign madness to conceal his identity, sail away to avoid the world, change his name and location three times, and withdraw after achieving success, which is the essence of righteousness. Even in matters such as the "Luxurious Well" and the "Pleasure Palace," he remained undistracted by luxury. The principles of rise and fall are not solely determined by fate, but also by personal ability. The fall of Wu by the state of Yue was due to the full use of diplomacy and flexibility, and the acquisition of rare and precious items from various dynasties, all of which, despite their different appearances, share the same underlying principles. Learned gentlemen, verify these words.

**Translation:** The Book of Lost Memories, Volume Four King Zhao of Yan (Five Matters) Two years after ascending the throne, King Zhao received two skilled dancers from the distant land of Guangyan: one named Xuan Juan and the other Ti Mo. Both had skin as smooth as jade, light as air, and exuded a fragrant aura. They were graceful and slender, unparalleled in ancient times. Their movements left no trace or shadow, and they could survive for years without food. King Zhao provided them with a luxurious silk tent and offered them precious liquid and special grains. Upon ascending the magnificent platform of Chong Xia, he summoned the two dancers, who performed a dance that was so enchanting it seemed they could not bear the weight of their own beauty. The king waved a silk ribbon at them, and they began to dance. Their appearance was so beautiful and alluring that it surpassed even the grace of a phoenix, and their singing was clear and melodious, with a flowing tone. He then had a female singer perform their song, which was so clear and resonant that it seemed to cause the beams and trees to move, but it was still not sufficient to praise their performance. Their dance had three names: "Ying Chen," which described how their light bodies seemed to intertwine with the dust; "Ji Yu," which described how their movements were as graceful as feathers dancing in the wind; and "Xuan Huai," which described how their limbs were so entwined and delicate that they seemed to enter the folds of one's sleeves.

King Zhao then arranged seating with a qilin pattern and scattered the fragrant substance of *Qunwu*. The fragrance came from the distant land of *Boyige*, and when it soaked into the ground, the soil and stones all became fragrant. It adhered to decaying wood and grass, and nothing failed to flourish. When used to fumigate dry bones, the flesh and muscles all grew back. When the powder was sprinkled on the ground, about four to five inches thick, two women could dance on it for a whole day without leaving any trace, because their bodies were light. Sometimes a white phoenix would fly alone, carrying thousands of ears of grain. The grain grew by itself in the air, and when the flowers and fruits fell to the ground, they sprouted roots and leaves. In one year, a hundred harvests were gathered, and one stalk filled a cart, hence it was called "abundant grains filling the cart." The qilin pattern was a mixture of precious materials used to decorate the seating, all in the shapes of clouds, rainbows, qilin, and phoenixes. King Zhao then waved his sleeve, and the dancers all stopped. Knowing of their supernatural nature, King Zhao had them reside on the high platform of the towering clouds, arranging pillows and seating for sleeping and feasting, and sending attendants to guard them. The king enjoyed the practices of immortals, and the daughters of the Heavenly Realm took the forms of these two women. At the end of King Zhao's reign, no one knew where they were. It is said they traveled along the Han River or along the banks of the Yi and Luo rivers.

In the fourth year, King Zhao resided in his main hall and summoned his minister Gan Xu, saying, "I have set my heart on the path of immortality and wish to learn the method of long life and enduring vision. Can I achieve this?" Gan Xu replied, "I once traveled to Mount Kuntuo and saw an elderly man with long white hair, who appeared as if he were a young boy, with a face as pure as ice and snow and a body as that of a maiden. His blood was clear and his bones strong, his skin firm and his intestines light. He had journeyed through the lands of Peng and Ying and beyond the blue sea, experiencing the ups and downs of the spiritual realm, traveling endlessly. This is a supreme immortal. He has the ability to eliminate desires and detach from attachments, to cleanse his spirit and extinguish his thoughts, and to frequently travel through the gates of Taiji. Now, Your Majesty is bewitched by beautiful appearances that deceive the eyes, indulges in delicious food that pleases the palate, and is surrounded by a group of beautiful women, bewiling your heart and confusing your mind. The beauty of the beloved is feared to be less than jade, and the slender waist and white teeth are worrying that they are not as divine. And yet you wish to resist aging and wander in the clouds. How is this different from measuring the vast ocean with a small measure or turning the sun and moon with a tiny tool? Can it be achieved?" King Zhao then abandoned colors and flavors, resided in his main hall, bestowed a robe made of feathers upon Gan Xu, and designated the place as "The Clarification of Truth."

In the fourth year, the King resided in his main chamber and summoned his minister Gan Xu, saying: "I have set my heart on the Way of the Immortals, and wish to learn the method of long life and eternal vision. Can this be achieved?" Gan Xu replied: "I once traveled to Mount KunTai, where I saw an old man with white hair, yet appearing as a young child, with a face as pure as snow and a form as delicate as a maiden. His blood was clear, his bones strong, his skin solid, and his intestines light. He had traversed the lands of Peng and Ying, surpassed the Azure Sea, and roamed through the rising and falling realms without end. This was a superior immortal. He was able to rid himself of clinging desires and detach from worldly attachments, purify his spirit and extinguish his thoughts, and frequently wander the Gate of the Supreme Ultimate. Now, Your Majesty is dazzled by alluring beauty, indulges in delicious food, and is surrounded by a crowd of virtuous women, clouding your mind and disturbing your thoughts. The beauty you cherish may not compare to jade, and the slender waist and white teeth you admire may not rival the gods. And yet you desire to defy aging and wander the clouds—how different is this from trying to measure the sea with a small jade cup, or turning the sun and moon by grasping a single hair? How can such a thing be achieved?" King Zhao then abandoned beauty, reduced his indulgence in taste, resided in his main chamber, bestowed upon Gan Xu a robe of feathers, and designated the site as "The Illumination of Truth."

In the seventh year, the nation of Muxu came to pay homage, which is another name for the nation of Shendu. A person named Shi practiced a certain art or skill. When asked about his age, he said, "One hundred and thirty years." He carried a staff and a bottle, and said, "It took me five years to travel from my country to Yan Capital." He was skilled in the art of deception and illusion. From the tips of his fingers emerged a ten-story pagoda, three feet high, along with various celestial beings and deities, all exquisitely beautiful and unique. The figures were all about five or six inches tall, arranged in a procession, drumming and dancing, circling the pagoda, and singing in a voice as real as that of living people. Shi exhaled water to create a mist that obscured visibility for several miles. Soon, he blew it away with a strong wind, dispersing the mist. He then blew the pagoda on his fingers, making it rise into the clouds. From his left ear emerged a green dragon, and from his right ear emerged a white tiger. Initially, they were only an inch or two long, but they grew to eight or nine feet in length. As the wind rose and the clouds gathered, he waved his hand, and the dragon and tiger disappeared back into his ears. He then opened his mouth toward the sun, and people riding on feathered canopies and driving mythical creatures entered his mouth. He pressed his hand against his chest, and a thunderous sound could be heard from within his sleeves. When he opened his mouth again, the people and creatures emerged from his mouth, following each other. Shi often sat in the sun, gradually feeling his form shrink, sometimes transforming into an old man, sometimes into an infant, dying suddenly, filling the room with fragrance, and occasionally being blown by a cool breeze, reborn in the same form as before. His spells and illusions were endless, and the supernatural phenomena were boundless.

In the eighth year, envoys from Lu Fu Guo came to pay homage, having traveled a distance of ten thousand miles across the river to arrive. They reported that in their country, the mountains and rivers were serene, with no evil beasts. The waters did not create waves, and the winds did not break trees. The people all lived for three hundred years, binding grass to make clothing, which is called "grass clothes." They remained eternally young until death, and all knew the values of filial piety and yielding. Those who lived beyond a hundred years respected each other as if they were closest relatives. They buried the dead in the wild, using fragrant wood and grass to cover the body. The community assisted in the funeral, and the sounds of weeping echoed through the forests and valleys, causing the river's source to cease flowing and the spring trees to change color. During the mourning period, they did not eat or drink until the bones of the deceased turned to dust, then they resumed eating. In ancient times, Yu the Great followed the mountains and guided the rivers, and thus he honored this land as the country of the ageless and pure filial piety. The record states: Those who contain spirit and receive breath, taking their form from the two instruments; receiving the mandate and thus living, encompassing the five virtues. Therefore, maintaining purity and clarity to guide one's actions, and using generosity as the foundation. Righteousness stemming from natural connections, showing love and respect throughout life; having a kind heart, even in death, there is a longing for the end. This is the constant nature of things and the common path of those who understand. Thus, when a loyal advisor speaks once, it brings understanding and illuminates the hidden; when righteousness comes from the heart, the spirits are moved and respond. The signs are clear and the spirits are evident, bringing down many good omens; following the path without deviation, both near and far, they are praised for their virtue. How admirable are the people of a foreign land, isolated from the king's influence, lacking knowledge of the great way. Describing their laws, there is a difference between the Chinese and the barbarians; observing their political and educational systems, they are quite different from the usual customs. Rites exist among the four barbarian regions, and these matters are recorded in the edicts; the spirit of filial piety and yielding is highly valued.

Nine years later, King Zhuang pondered the various divine wonders. A Daoist scholar named Gu Zang spoke to the king, saying, "The Queen Mother of the West will come to visit, and she will surely speak of the techniques of emptiness." Within a year, the Queen Mother indeed arrived. She wandered with King Zhuang in the forest of firewood, discussing the method of making fire by drilling, attributed to the Flame Emperor. They collected the resin of the green osmanthus and burned it to light the night. Suddenly, a moth appeared, carrying a flame, its appearance like that of a red bird, and it touched the resin. This moth emerged from a cave on Mount Yuanqiu. The cave penetrated the highest heavens, and inside were tiny pearls like flowing sand, which could be pierced and knotted, used as ornaments, and were the arrows of the divine moth. The moth relied on qi and drank dew, never landing, and was killed by immortals to make elixirs. The Queen Mother and the immortals gathered divine moths, placed them in a jade basket, and had a jade boy carry it to wander the four corners of the earth. They descended to the court of Yan and showed the moth to King Zhuang. The king said, "I request this moth to make the Nine Transformation Elixir!" But the Queen Mother did not give it to him.

King Zhao sat on the *Worri Sun* platform, which reached into the clouds, and from where one could touch the sun. At that time, a bird with a black body and white head came to the king's place, carrying a pearl that illuminated darkness, with a diameter of one foot. This pearl was black like lacquer and illuminated the room when hung inside, with no gods or spirits able to conceal its spirit. This pearl came from the bottom of a dark spring, located in the north of Cold Mountain, in a round body of water, where the water waves always flowed in a circular motion. There was a black abalone that flew, coming and going among the Five Great Mountains. In the time of the Yellow Emperor, the hermit Fog Chengzi traveled the peaks of Cold Mountain and found the black abalone on a high cliff, thus knowing that the black abalone could fly. By the time of King Zhao of Yan, a state presented the pearl to King Zhao. The king washed the sand and mud with water from the Jade River and then exclaimed, "Since the time of the sun and moon, I have seen the black abalone produce pearls eighty to ninety times, and this abalone produces a pearl once every thousand years." The pearl gradually became lighter and finer. King Zhao often carried this pearl, and during the hottest months, his body felt cool and light, and it was called the "Pearl that Dispels Heat and Attracts Coolness."

The First Emperor of Qin (Four Events) In the first year of the First Emperor's reign, a skilled artist from the country of Qianxiao was offered to him. This artist could create images on the ground by rinsing it with cinnabar and verdigris, instantly forming pictures of mythical and monstrous creatures. He also carved jade into the shapes of a hundred beasts, with fur so lifelike that it seemed real. All his works were inscribed on their chests, marked with the date and month. He drew on the ground with his finger, creating a hundred-paces-long line as straight as a string and plumb line. Within a square inch, he could draw maps of the four rivers, five mountains, and various states. He also drew dragons and phoenixes, soaring as if flying. None of his drawings could have their eyes painted, or if they were, they would fly away. The First Emperor sighed and said, "How can the carved images fly away?" He ordered them to paint the eyes of two jade tigers with pure lacquer. After ten days, the tigers were lost, and their whereabouts were unknown. The people of the mountains and marshes reported, "We saw two white tigers, each missing one eye, walking together, with similar fur colors, different from what is commonly seen." The following year, two white tigers, each missing one eye, were offered from the west. The First Emperor opened the cage to look at them, suspecting they were the ones previously lost, and then had them killed. He checked their chests, and indeed they were the jade tigers carved in the first year. Until the death of Hu Hai, the precious swords and divine objects were scattered at the time.

The First Emperor was fond of matters related to immortals and immortality. People from the land of Wanyu arrived by means of a shell-like boat. The boat's shape resembled a shell, and it could travel underwater without being submerged. It was also known as a "Lunbo Boat." The people from this country were exceptionally tall, each being ten zhang (approximately 30 meters) tall. They covered their bodies with the feathers and fur of birds and beasts. The First Emperor conversed with them and discussed the creation of the world, as if he had personally witnessed it. One of them said, "When I was young, I could walk on air and travel vast distances in a day. Now, while seated, I can see beyond the known world. My country is ten million li away from the mountain of Xuanyuan (Yellow Emperor's) and considers a year to be equivalent to a single day in our time. Our country is often shrouded in mist, but when the sun shines, the sky clears and the clouds part, revealing a brightness like the rivers and lakes. Then, there appear black dragons and phoenixes, flying down from the sky. At night, we use stones to provide light. These stones come from a mountain called Ranshan, and the soil and stones there emit light naturally. When struck, the stones break into small pieces, resembling millet grains, and a single piece can illuminate an entire hall. In ancient times, the Yellow Emperor used this fire to transform raw food into cooked food. Now, the people of our country present this stone to you. If one were to throw this stone into a stream, boiling foam would flow for tens of miles, and the water would be named 'Jiao Yuan.' My country is ten million li away from the mountain of Xuanyuan (Yellow Emperor's). The son of Shao Dian mined copper from Mount Shou and cast three great cauldrons. I sensed the movement of metal and fire in my country and rushed to witness the completion of the three cauldrons. I also saw unusual energy in Yizhou, indicating the birth of a sage, which turned out to be Emperor Yao. Additionally, I saw red clouds entering Feng and Hao, indicating the appearance of auspicious signs of prosperity." The First Emperor said, "These are divine beings." He became even more convinced of the art of immortality.

The First Emperor was fond of matters concerning immortals and spirits. There were people from Wanchu who arrived by riding a snail-shaped boat. The boat was shaped like a snail, and it traveled beneath the sea without water entering it; it was also called the "Wave-Sinking Boat." The people of this land were ten zhang tall, and they wove the feathers and fur of birds and beasts to conceal their forms. The First Emperor conversed with them, and they spoke of the time when heaven and earth first opened, describing it as clearly as if he had seen it with his own eyes. They said: "When I was young, I walked on empty space and moved backward, traveling ten thousand li in a single day. Now, in my old age, I sit and see matters beyond the heavens and earth. My country lies nine ten-thousand li beyond the place where the sun sets beyond the Sea of Salt. There, a single day is equal to ten thousand years. The customs there are often shrouded in mist and fog, but when the sun shines, the sky clears and the clouds split open, revealing a brilliance as bright as the rivers Han and Jiang. At such times, black dragons and dark phoenixes soar and descend from above. When night comes, stones are burned to continue the daylight. These stones come from the Flame Mountain; the soil and rocks there all emit their own light, and when struck, they break into pieces like millet grains, each one illuminating an entire hall. In ancient times, Emperor Yan first transformed raw food into cooked food using this fire. The people of my land now offer this stone to you. If someone were to cast such a stone into a stream or ravine, boiling foam would flow for dozens of li, and the water would be named the "Scorched Abyss." My country lies ten thousand li away from the Hill of Xuanyuan. The son of Shao Dian mined copper from the Mountain of Shou and cast it into great tripods. I once saw signs of movement of golden and fiery qi from that land, and rushed to see it, only to find the three tripods already completed. I also saw strange qi over the state of Ji, which foretold the birth of a sage; indeed, Qingdu gave birth to Yao. I also saw red clouds entering the region of Feng and Hao, and went to see it, only to find the auspicious sign of the vermilion bird bringing prosperity." The First Emperor said, "This is a divine being." From then on, he placed even greater faith in the arts of immortality.

Qin Shi Huang built the Cloudy Bright Platform, gathering the finest trees from all directions and seeking out the most skilled craftsmen from across the land. From the south, he obtained smoky hills with green trees, the Li River with burning sand, red clay from Bendi, and white bamboo from Yun Gang; from the east, he obtained lush mountains with colorful cypress trees, floating and drifting dragon pines, cold rivers with star-shaped zelkova, and Chinese catalpa from the cloudy mountains; from the west, he obtained leaking gold from the sea, feathered jade from Wolf Abyss, clearing cliffs with sunset mulberry, and sinking ponds with round tally; from the north, he obtained dark hills with dry lacquer, shady slopes with patterned Chinese tamarisk, lifting flow with black soul, and dark sea with fragrant jade, all precious and rare items were gathered. Two people soared through the void and climbed trees, swinging axes in the air, starting work at midnight and completing it by noon. The people of Qin called it the "Subei Platform," or it was said that in the Subei area, each built a platform, and both explanations are doubtful.

Zhang Yi and Su Qin, both having the same aspirations and loving learning, took turns cutting their hair and selling it to support each other. Sometimes they worked as hired laborers to write books, but they would not read anything unless it was the words of sages. When they encountered the *Fen* and *Dian* (ancient texts), they would inscribe them on their palms and inner thighs with ink, then write them down at night. They split bamboo to make writing tablets. Whenever they ate on the road, they would peel tree bark and weave it into book covers to hold the good books of the world. Once, they rested under a large tree and fell asleep. An old man asked them, "Why are you so hardworking and diligent?" Zhang Yi and Su Qin asked him, "What country are you from?" He replied, "I was born in Guigǔ." It is also known as Guigǔ, where "鬼" means "return" or "come back." Another interpretation is that Guigǔ is the name of a valley. They then asked him for his skills, and he taught them how to debate and argue in the world. They found two scrolls of persuasive writing inside his chest, which contained advice for the times. The *Ancient History Examination* states, "He was also known as Guigǔ, where '鬼' means 'return' or 'come back.' Another interpretation is that Guigǔ is the name of a valley." Prince Ziying of Qin ascended the throne, but after one hundred days, the eunuch Zhao Gao plotted to kill him.

Ziying resided in the Palace of Wangyi, where one night he dreamt of a figure whose body was ten zhang long, with hair and beard of an extraordinary green hue. The figure wore jade shoes and rode in a red carriage, driven by vermilion horses, arriving at the palace gates. Declaring that he wished to see the prince of Qin, the gatekeeper allowed him to enter. Ziying then engaged in conversation with the figure, who said to Ziying, "I am an envoy from heaven, coming from Shachou. The world is about to descend into chaos, and there will be people with the same name who will engage in violent conflict." The next day, Ziying awoke, and he began to suspect Zhao Gao. He imprisoned Zhao Gao in the prison of Xianyang, suspending him in a well, but he did not die after seven days. They then boiled him in a large pot of water, but it did not boil after seven days, and only then did they kill him. Ziying asked the prison officials, "Is Zhao Gao a god?" The officials replied, "When we first imprisoned Zhao Gao, we saw him carrying a green pill as large as a sparrow's egg." At the time, Daoist practitioners said, "Zhao Gao's ancestors received elixir techniques from Han Zhong, allowing him to sit on frozen ice in winter and lie on a stove in summer, unaware of the cold or heat." After Zhao Gao's death, Ziying abandoned his body at a crossroads, and thousands of families wept as they sent him off. Some saw a green bird emerge from Zhao Gao's body and fly directly into the clouds. The nine-fold transformation, indeed, was proven true in this case. Ziying's dream was the spirit of Qin Shi Huang; the jade shoes he wore were left behind by Master Anqi. The principles of ghosts and spirits are rare and significant, occurring once in ten thousand generations.

录曰：夫含灵挺质，罕不羡乎久视，祈以长生。苟乖才性，企之弥远。何者？夫层宫峻宇肆其奢，绰约柔曼纵其惑，《九韶》、《六度》悦其耳，喜怒刑赏示其威，精灵溺于常滞，志意疲于驰策，销竭神虑，翦刻天和。秦正自以功高三皇，世逾五帝，取惑徐市，身殒沙丘。燕昭能延礼群神，百灵响集。并欲弃机事以游真极，去尘垢而望云飞。譬犹等沟浍于天河，齐朝菌于椿木，超二仪于昆峦，升一匮而扳重汉。何则望之与无阶矣。《抱朴子》曰：“学若牛毛，得如麟角。”至如秦皇、燕昭之智，虽微鉴仙体，而未入玄真。 Translation: The record states: Sentient beings, possessing a soul and a natural form, rarely do not envy long life and pray for eternal existence. If one's talent and nature are not in harmony, the more one strives, the farther it seems. Why is this so? Because grand palaces and tall buildings indulge in luxury, graceful and gentle beauty indulges in temptation, the ancient musical compositions "Jiu Shao" and "Liu Du" please the ears, and the display of joy, anger, rewards, and punishments shows authority. The spirit is trapped in mundane entanglements, and the will and aspirations are exhausted by constant striving, leading to the exhaustion of mental and spiritual energy and the cutting away of natural harmony. Qin Shi Huang, believing his achievements surpassed those of the Three Sovereigns and his reign exceeded that of the Five Emperors, was deceived by Xu Shi and died at Shaoqiu. Yan Zhaowang, known for his ability to attract and honor various deities, had a hundred spirits respond and gather. Both wished to abandon worldly affairs and seek the ultimate truth, leaving behind the dirt and dust to aspire to fly like clouds. It is like equating small streams with the Milky Way, morning mushrooms with cypress trees, transcending the two fundamental principles among the mountains, and climbing one step to reach the distant stars. Why is it as if there is no way to achieve it? The *Baopu Zi* says, "Learning is as numerous as the hairs on a cow, but those who succeed are as rare as the horn of a unicorn." Even the intelligence of Qin Shi Huang and Yan Zhaowang, though they had a slight understanding of the divine body, did not enter the realm of the profound truth.

There are still many narrow-minded and confused people, and their emotions are not fully resolved. As for supernatural transformations and myriad changes, it is said that companions who move slowly like clouds hanging in the sky and those who ride shadows and clouds can be compared in status and can be associated with them. They come from all directions, displaying extraordinary and unique forms, arriving from distant places. They rely on the supernatural to achieve complete transformation and use transformation to exhaust the supernatural, touching upon phenomena that are hard to name, mysterious and unpredictable. The *Huainanzi* states: "The art of containing thunder and spewing fire originates from the family of Wan Bi." To place downy feathers in a great furnace and fire and smoke in ice water, ships that leak into the sea, pearls flying and clouds sinking, thousands of paths and myriad kinds, details not fully explained in books, since the era of deification, miraculous and unmatched, how could they be imitated by those with short lives and limited knowledge? Now, observing the records of Zi Nian, Su and Zhang, two people, have different words but the same deeds, sometimes due to similar sounds of characters, sometimes due to different local customs, verifying in ancient texts, how could it be only that Qin and Yi were seen as different?

**Translation:** The *Shiyi Ji* Volume V Han Dynasty, Part I When the Taishang Huang of Han was in humble times, he wore a dagger, three feet long, with inscriptions on it that were difficult to read. It was suspected to have been made during the time when King Wu Ding of Yin (Shang) was waging war against the tribe of Guifang. The Taishang Huang was traveling in the mountains of Feng and Pei. In a remote valley, there was someone smelting and casting metal. The Taishang Huang rested nearby and asked, "What are you casting?" The worker laughed and replied, "I am casting a sword for the Son of Heaven. Be careful not to reveal this!" The Taishang Huang thought it was a joke and showed no sign of doubt. The worker said, "The iron and steel we are casting is difficult to refine. If we mix in your dagger from your waist, it will become a divine weapon capable of conquering the world. Star spirits will assist, and we can defeat the three cunning individuals. The decline of wood and the dominance of fire are signs of an extraordinary omen." The Taishang Huang said, "This dagger is called a *bishou* (dagger-axe). Its sharpness is unparalleled; it can cut through dragons in water and tigers on land. No spirits or monsters can withstand it. It can cut jade and engrave gold without dulling its edge."

The craftsman said, "If one does not obtain this dagger to blend and cast, even if Ou Yi were to focus his expertise, sharpen the edge with a fine grindstone, and fold the blade, it would still be a coarse tool." The emperor then removed the dagger and threw it into the furnace. Soon the smoke and flames rose to the sky, making the day as dark as night. When the sword was completed, three animals were slaughtered to consecrate it. The craftsman asked the emperor, "When did you obtain this dagger?" The emperor replied, "During the reign of King Zhaoxiang of Qin, I encountered a rustic person who gave it to me on the road, saying it was a divine object from the Shang Dynasty, passed down through generations, with ancient characters on it, recording the year and month." When the sword was completed, the craftsman examined it, and the inscription was still there, confirming the earlier doubts. The craftsman then held the sword and presented it to the emperor. The emperor bestowed it upon Gaozu, who wore it on his person to eliminate the three wicked ones. When the world was pacified, Empress Lü hid it in the treasure house. The guard in the treasure house saw white vapor like clouds, emanating from outside the door, resembling dragons and snakes. Empress Lü renamed the treasure house "Spiritual Gold Repository." When the relatives of Lü gained power, the white vapor also disappeared. When Emperor Hui ascended the throne, this treasure house stored forbidden weapons, and it was called "Spiritual Gold Inner Treasury."

The Record states: The transformations of spirits are manifold; the profound feelings that transcend the ordinary are inherently difficult to maintain. As for the records in the *Fen* (a text on divination) and the prophecies, they all serve as proof of past events; folk songs and simple sayings all seek evidence of future occurrences. By examining maps and studying records, one often finds them compiled and listed. Considering the sayings of craftsmen, one can believe in the far-reaching effects of such prophecies. A sword three feet long corresponds to the numbers of heaven and earth. Therefore, the number three is a yang number, corresponding to the virtues of heaven and earth. According to the *Gouming Jue* (a classic text), it says: "Xiao He is the essence of the star Altair, while Xiang Yu, Chen Sheng, and Hu Hai are the three cunning individuals." The state is governed by the virtue of wood, and the Han dynasty by the virtue of fire, which is evident in this. In the second year of Emperor Hui's reign, all directions praised the unification of writing and cart tracks, indicating a peaceful and harmonious world. Distant and foreign lands sent tributes through multiple translations. At that time, there was a Daoist named Han Zhi, a descendant of Han Zhong (a legendary figure). He came across the sea, claiming to be a messenger from the Eastern Sea, and having heard of the emperor's virtue spreading throughout the land, he willingly submitted himself. There was a land called Nili beyond the Land of the Rising Sun, whose people were four feet tall, had two horns like cocoons, and teeth protruding from their lips. Since birth, they had a divine hair that provided protection, lived in deep caves, and their lifespan could not be measured. The emperor said, "The Daoist Han Zhi understands the language of the distant foreigners. Let him ask how long their life is and what they have seen across several generations." The answer was, "The Five Elements succeed each other, with life and death alternating, like flying dust and fine rain, making existence and death impossible to calculate."

The young man asked, "Is anything known about the time before Nüwa?" The other replied, "Above the serpent's body, the eight winds are balanced, the four seasons are in order, and the essence of the universe operates without the need for force or pleasure." When asked about the time before Suiren, the response was, "Since the discovery of fire and the cooking of food, fathers were loving and sons were long-lived and filial. Since the era of Huangdi, there has been constant warfare, a decadent and restless society, excessive and disorderly rituals, and the decline of moral values and pure customs." The young man reported these answers to the emperor. The emperor said, "How distant and obscure! Only those who understand profound truths can discuss such matters. This is far beyond the reach of ordinary people." The young man then withdrew, and no one knew where he went. The emperor ordered various scholars to build a sacred platform in the north of Chang'an, naming it "Cishi Hall." Common belief held that the god of cold was worshipped in the shadow of the city. According to the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, it was said that offerings were made to the god of cold, and the pronunciation was similar, but it was definitely "Cishi Hall." In the second year, the emperor decreed that a hundred palace ladies, ten thousand bolts of brocade, and ten ships were to be sent as gifts to a messenger to Mudili, and a general amnesty was declared.

Emperor Wu of Han deeply missed Lady Li, whom he could no longer see. At that time, he had just excavated the Kunling Pool and was boating on a vessel adorned like a flying bird. The emperor composed his own songs and had a female singer perform them. As the sun had already set and a cool wind stirred the water, the female singer's voice was very powerful, and he composed the song "Falling Leaves and Mourning Cicadas," which went: "Silent are the sleeves, dust rises on the jade steps. The empty room is cold and lonely, fallen leaves cling to the heavy doors. How can I see that beautiful woman again? My heart is still unsettled!" The emperor was deeply moved by the song, feeling depressed and unable to bear it, and ordered a lamp made of dragon fat to illuminate the boat, unable to stop his sadness. His attendants noticed his face was filled with sorrow and resentment, and they presented wine from Hongliang, served in cups made of seashells. These cups came from the country of Boqi, and the wine came from the county of Hongliang, which belonged to the Right Commandery. Until Emperor Ai abolished this town, southern people learned this brewing method. Now people say "Yunyang produces fine wine," but the two sounds have become confused. The emperor drank three cups, his face brightened, and his heart was joyful, and he ordered the female singer to attend. He rested in the Cool Extension Room and dreamed that Lady Li gave him the fragrance of *hengwu*. He woke up, and the fragrance still lingered on his clothes and pillow, lasting for months. The emperor continued to long for her, but she never appeared again, and he wept until the mat was wet, and he changed the Cool Extension Room to the Fragrance of Remembrance Room. Initially, the emperor deeply favored Lady Li, and after her death, he often thought of and dreamed of her, sometimes wanting to see her. He looked emaciated, and his consorts were uneasy.

The emperor issued an edict to summon Li Shaojun and spoke with him, saying, "I miss Lady Li; can I see her again?" Li Shaojun replied, "She can be seen from afar but not in the same room. In the Dark Sea, there is a stone called the Hidden Pearl, which is blue in color and as light as feathers. When it is cold, the stone is warm; when it is hot, the stone is cool. Carving it into a human likeness, it has a divine understanding no different from a real person. If this stone figure goes, then Lady Li will come. This stone figure can translate human speech, having sound but no breath, thus proving its supernatural nature." The emperor said, "Can I get this stone figure?" Li Shaojun replied, "I wish to have a hundred ships, a thousand strong men, and those who can swim and climb trees, all skilled in Daoist arts, bringing the elixir of immortality." They went to the Dark Sea and returned after ten years. Those who went before either ascended to the clouds and did not return, or took on false forms and pretended to die; only four or five returned. When they obtained the stone, they ordered the craftsmen to carve it according to the original design into the likeness of Lady Li. Once carved, it was placed behind a sheer curtain, looking just like her when alive. The emperor was very pleased and asked Li Shaojun, "Can I get close to her?" Li Shaojun said, "It's like having a dream in the middle of the night; can you see it clearly during the day? This stone is toxic; it's best to look at it from a distance and not get too close. Do not underestimate the majesty of the emperor; be wary of this supernatural entity!" The emperor then followed his advice. After seeing Lady Li, Li Shaojun had the stone figure ground into pills, which were taken, and the emperor no longer dreamed of her. They then built a Spirit Dream Terrace and worshipped it regularly.

In the first year of the Yuánfēng era, the country of Fúxīn presented the mud of lánjīn gold. This gold comes from the hot springs, during the hottest part of summer, the water often boils and surges, as if it were boiling water or fire, so intense that birds cannot fly over it. The locals often see people working by the water to make objects from this gold, the gold appearing as if it were mud, with a color like polished purple; after many castings, its color turns white, shining like silver, which is known as the "silver candle." This mud is commonly used to seal various chests and palace doors, and ghosts and evil spirits dare not approach. During the Han dynasty, high-ranking officials going to war or on missions to distant lands often used this mud to seal imperial seals. The envoys of Wei Qing, Zhang Qian, Su Wu, and Fu Jiezi all received seals sealed with this gold mud. After the death of Emperor Wu, this mud ceased to be used. To the south of Rìnnán, there is a pond called Yínquán. It is said that the water seeps out of the ground and forms a deep pool, hence the name "Yínquán." Some say that this water is sweet and soft, and if men and women drink it, they become lustful. In small areas, it can be easily crossed, and in larger areas, boats can navigate along its course, following its winding path. The sound of the water hitting the rocks is like human singing and laughter, and those who hear it feel a sense of desire, hence the common name "Yínquán." Occasionally, there are ducks and geese with a golden color, flying in groups and playing on the sandy banks. A traveler once saw a golden duck flying south and arriving at Yínquán. Later, in the first year of the Bǎodǐng era, Zhang Shan served as the governor of Rìnnán, and a local resident found and presented a golden duck to him. Zhang Shan was knowledgeable and virtuous, and upon examining the date, he confirmed that it was the golden duck from the tomb of Emperor Qin Shi Huang.

In the past, Qin Shi Huang constructed his tomb, gathering rare and precious things from all over the world, and burying living workers alive with him. He filled the tomb with exotic treasures from distant lands, creating an interior modeled after rivers, seas, lakes, and mountains. He used sandalwood and fragrant wood to make boats and paddles, gold and silver to make ducks and geese, and glass and various precious materials to make turtles and fish. In the sea, he created jade elephants and whales, holding fire pearls as stars to replace oil lamps, with light shining out of the tomb, showcasing the grandeur of the spiritual aspect. When the tomb was opened, the workers who had been buried alive were found to be still alive. These workers carved stone images of dragons, phoenixes, immortals, and wrote inscriptions and praises inside the tomb. When the tomb was opened at the beginning of the Han dynasty, it was checked against historical records, and there were no records of such divine figures. It was concluded that these carvings and inscriptions were the work of the surviving workers. Later, the inscription was rewritten, containing many words of resentment and cruelty, and it was called the "Resentment Stele." The *Records of the Grand Historian* briefly mentioned it but did not record it in detail.

Dong Yan often slept in the room of Yan Qing, using painted stones as a bed, the patterns like brocade. The stone was very light, coming from the country of Zhizhi. Above, there were purple glass curtains and fire-screened screens, with candles made of lingma (a type of plant), and a purple jade plate shaped like a coiled dragon, all adorned with various treasures. The attendant fanned Dong Yan outside the door. Dong Yan asked, "Do jade and stone need to be fanned to cool down?" The attendant then stopped fanning and touched it, only then realizing there was a screen. There was also a jade plate, storing ice in front of his knees. The jade plate and the ice were equally pure and transparent. The attendant thought that without a plate, the ice would melt and wet the mat, so he tried to sweep it with the jade plate, but it fell down the steps, shattering both the ice and the jade, which Dong Yan found amusing. This jade plate was a gift from the country of Qiantu. Emperor Wu gave this to Dong Yan. During the reigns of Emperors Ai and Ping, common households still had this item, but it was mostly broken. By the time of Wang Mang, it was no longer known where it was.

During the second year of the Tai' An era, the Great Yuezhi country presented a double-headed chicken to the Han court. This chicken had four feet and one tail, and both heads would crow together. Emperor Wu placed the chicken in the old Pavilion of Ganquan and mixed it with other chickens, but the others could not produce the same sound. Advisers said, "The *Book of Songs* states: 'A hen does not crow at dawn.' Another version says, 'If a hen crows at dawn, it is a sign of household decline.' Now that the male chickens do not crow, this is not a good omen." The emperor then sent the chicken back to the Western Regions. As it traveled to the Western Pass, the chicken looked back at the Han palace and mournfully crowed. Therefore, there was a folk saying which said: "In the chaotic end times, chickens do not crow, dogs do not bark, thorns and brambles grow in the palace, and nine tigers will vie for the throne." When Wang Mang usurped the throne, his generals were known by the title "Nine Tigers." Afterward, chaos and disorder increased, with thorns and brambles growing in the palace and no chickens crowing or dogs barking in the households. Before the chicken reached the Yuezhi country, it flew into the Milky Way, sounding like a *kun* chicken and soaring through the clouds. It is also known as a *xuan* chicken, with the sounds *kun* and *xuan* being similar.

In the second year of the Tianhan era (101 BCE), in the west of the Chushou Kingdom, there was the Qilun Kingdom. Its customs were pure and harmonious, and the people lived for three hundred years. There was a forest of longevity trees, each tree reaching a height of a thousand fathoms, hiding the sun and moon. If one rested under these trees, they would neither fall ill nor die. Some who crossed seas and mountains to visit this kingdom brought back leaves, and those who kept them would never age. The people of the kingdom wove grass and hair into ropes and made clothing from nets, similar to the fine silk of today. In the sixth year of the Yuanshou era (101 BCE), the Chushou Kingdom presented a set of net clothing. The emperor burned it on a major thoroughfare, fearing that future generations would covet such luxury, and the smoke resembled the vapor of precious metals and stones. In the second year of the Taishi era (95 BCE), in the west, there was the Yin Xiao Kingdom, where the people were all skilled at whistling. Men could be heard up to a hundred miles away, and women up to fifty miles away, with a sound like that of a sheng and a yu. In autumn and winter, the sound was clear and bright; in spring and summer, it was deep and low. The tip of their tongue was inverted into the throat, also called two tongues overlapping, and if gently scratched with nails, the whistling sound would travel even farther. Therefore, the *Spring and Autumn Annals of Mr. Lü* states, "A country with inverted tongues in different regions," referring to this. If there were a most holy ruler, they would be influenced by their culture.

The Han dynasty rose, inheriting the legacy of the six states' misfortunes, and the emperor aspired to virtue. Thus, the common people lamented the late fall of Qin and regretted the tardiness of the Han dynasty's arrival. Emperor Gaozu laid the foundation for imperial rule and expanded the territory. Emperor Hui focused on lenient laws, achieving a rule of non-action, with moral virtue equal to the three kings and teachings spreading throughout the world. By the time of Emperor Wu, the dynasty's legacy became even more illustrious. He inspected the provinces and visited the mountains, establishing the supreme title. He promoted rites and music to revitalize the customs and expanded the meaning of literature to adorn the customs. He reformed the calendar and laws and established the Fengshan sacrifices, worshipping all the gods to attract various blessings. Although the virtue of "reverence and clarity" is prominent in the *Book of Tang* and the virtue of "literary wisdom" is praised in the *Book of Yu*, how can one surpass this? Observing the teachings of Confucius and other sages, they do not value the study of the void. Emperor Wu studied the teachings of Huang-Lao, sought methods to avoid aging, and sought blessings from rituals that lacked merit. Thus, Zhang Chang spoke frankly, urging him to distance himself from the art of immortality, pointing to the examples of Chang Hong, Chu Xianghui, Qin Shi Huang, and Xu Fu. Therefore, the followers of Xin Yuan were ultimately executed. Immortals value tranquility and forget the body, maintaining solitude and eliminating worldly affairs. Emperor Wu favored secret travels and valued conquests, expanding the palace and gardens. He forever deviated from the method of long life and eternal vision, losing the essence of adhering to the Dao. He regretted the early execution of Shao Weng and was deceived by the deceptive words of Luan Da.

As for Lady Li, she cherished with deep affection, devoted herself to flattering the inner chamber, thinking of the soul's rebirth, and prepared a new palace to await. It is as if the excessive favor of a beloved has become too intense, and the knots of the heart have not been untied. She wanted to ascend above the clouds and rainbows, to be equal to heaven and earth, but it is like trying to catch the wind or measure time with a sundial; how can it be achieved? Although not reaching the level of supreme truth, she to some extent participated in the profound mysteries. Therefore, the profound and subtle cannot be hidden, and all things cannot conceal their spirits. Examining among the ranks of immortals and verifying with various sayings, there is nothing different from this! The five elements take turns in rise, the constant principle of numbers, the signs of the Metal and Earth elements, Wei and Jin dynasties should be here. Dong Yan rose from a pearl-selling background, he gained favor through the kitchen and dining hall, secretly enjoying a momentary favor, wealth that could overturn the world, possessing within rare and mystical treasures, displaying treasures that are not of this world; one day, the favor was cut off, is it a sign of the rise and fall? The purpose of making coffins is to prevent the harm of ants and termites, to temporarily gather the bones. The sage commands the proper rites, dislikes excessive spending, and dislikes being too thin. As for Dan Tai Mie Ming's frugality, the extravagance of King Sheng Ji and Emperor Qin Shi Huang, all miss the point of moderation. Alas! The body decays and the spirit perishes, suddenly becomes the soil of a coffin, becomes a hill and then a valley, precious jade and beautiful treasures, suddenly become ashes and dust. This is to waste life and add to death, it is of no benefit to one's reputation. To go away forever in darkness, how can one remember the past glory? Confucius said, "it is better to decay quickly." To gather the hands and feet, the sage uses this as a warning, isn't it commendable?

**Translation:** The *Shiyi Ji* (拾遗记) Volume Six Western Han Dynasty, Part Two In the first year of the Shiyuan era during Emperor Zhao's reign, a pool was dug, extending a thousand steps in width. In the center, lotus flowers with multiple branches were planted, each stem bearing four leaves, resembling a double canopy. When the sun shone, the leaves bent to shade the root and stem, like the sunflower protecting its base, and were named "Low Light Lotus." The fruits were like black pearls and could be used for decoration or as pendants. The flowers and leaves were difficult to wither, and their fragrant aroma could be smelled for more than ten miles. Eating them made one's breath always fragrant and benefited the body, treating illnesses. The palace ladies highly valued them, and whenever they went out for banquets or outings, they always chewed on them. Some cut them to make clothes, others broke them to shield themselves from the sun, for amusement. As mentioned in the *Chuci* (Songs of Chu), "cutting lotus leaves to make clothes," it means this. There were also upside-down-growing water lilies, with stems like tangled threads, one flower with a thousand petals, roots floating on the water, and fruits sinking in the mud, named "Purple Water Lily." Eating it prevented aging. The emperor ordered water-based entertainment, and they banqueted and entertained for the whole day. A local presented a large trough, and the emperor said, "A boat with a paddle made of cassia and a hull made of pine is still heavy and crude; how much more so, then, for this trough, can it be used?" He then ordered to use fine sandalwood for the boat and magnolia for the rudder. Flying phoenixes and swimming cranes were carved and decorated at the bow of the ship. Drifting gently with the wind, they forgot to return until the end of the day, even till the night. The emperor ordered the palace ladies to sing: "In the clear autumn light, drifting on the vast waves, With delicate hands, cutting lotus leaves. Cool winds blow, raising the oar songs. Clouds clear, dawn moon low over the river. A thousand years of joy, how could it be too much!" The emperor was greatly pleased and built a commercial pavilion on the pool. By the end of the reign, many advised against it, and he then reduced the frequency of outings, dismantling the pools and pavilions. The phoenix boats and lotus leaves were abandoned over time. Now the pavilion has no trace, and the pools and ditches are flat.

In the first year of the Diji era during the reign of Emperor Xuan, to the east of Lelang, there was a land known as the "Land of the Backward Light," which came to pay tribute with its local products. It was said that this land was located to the east of Fusang, where the sun rises in the west. The country is perpetually dark, suitable for cultivating all grains, and is named "Rongze," spanning three thousand li. All the grains are of high quality, but eating them leads to death shortly after. There is a type of rice called "Jia Ri Rice" that matures in ten days; another type, "Fan Xing Rice," is said to cause the eater to die and be reborn, thus extending their lifespan; there is also "Ming Qing Rice," which grants longevity to those who eat it; and "Qing Chang Rice," which provides long-lasting nourishment with just one grain. There is "Yao Zhi Millet," whose branches are long and weak, swaying constantly even without wind, and is said to strengthen the marrow when eaten; "Feng Guan Millet," which resembles a phoenix's crown, and is said to increase strength when consumed; "You Long Millet," whose leaves twist like a dragon; and "Qiong Gao Millet," which is white and cold, and is said to make bones light when eaten. There are also "Rao Ming Beans," whose weak stems intertwine; "Jia Jian Beans," whose pods resemble a person carrying a sword, growing sideways; and "Qing Li Beans," which lean over when exposed to sunlight, and are said to prevent aging and illness. There is "Yan Jing Wheat," which extends life and vitality; "Kun He Wheat," which harmonizes the six organs; "Qing Xin Wheat," which makes the body light; "Chun He Wheat," which, when made into wine, causes prolonged intoxication, and is said to allow one to go without clothing even in winter; and "Han Lu Wheat," which has grains containing dew, with a sweet taste like honey. There is "Zi Chen Hemp," whose seeds do not float; "Yun Bing Hemp," whose seeds are cold and have a lustrous appearance, suitable for oil production; and "Tong Ming Hemp," which allows one to walk at night without a torch. This is "Ju Lei," and eating it extends life, making one live longer than others.

To the north, there is a grass known as Rainbow Grass, with branches that stretch one zhang in length, leaves resembling carriage wheels, roots as large as a wheel hub, and flowers the color of a morning rainbow. In ancient times, Duke Huan of Qi attacked the Shanrong, and the people of the state presented its seeds, which were then planted in the courtyard, said to be an omen of a hegemon. There is also a grass called Night Brightness, which at night appears like rows of candles, but during the day has no light and disappears by itself. There is a purple chrysanthemum known as Solar Essence, with one stem and one vine extending over several mu, its taste being sweet, and those who eat it will never feel hungry or thirsty. There is a kind of burnt reed, five zhang high, which when burned turns to ash, and when water is poured on it, it turns back into reed, called Divine Reed. There is also a yellow channel grass that reflects sunlight like fire, hard and resilient like metal, and those who eat it do not feel heat even if they are burned. There is a dream grass, with leaves like rushes and stems like yarrow, collected to predict good or bad fortune, and it never fails. There is also a distant-scented grass, whose consumers have keen hearing, it smells like cassia, and its stem is like orchid. It was presented to the country, but many did not bear fruit, and the leaves often withered and turned yellow, so the emperor ordered them to be removed. In the second year of Yuan Feng, a platform was built south of Linchi to observe distant qi. It drew water from Taiye to the east. There was a tree with intertwined branches, with upper branches spanning the water channel and lower branches extending across the bank to the south, growing from the same trunk as the upper branches. The emperor often sailed in a boat named Henglan Yunyi during the last month of autumn, fishing at the platform all night long. He used fragrant gold as a hook, frost threads as a line, and red carp as bait, catching a white dragon, three zhang long, resembling a large snake without scales or armor. The emperor said, "It is not an auspicious sign," and ordered the imperial kitchen to make it into dried fish, which was purple meat and green bones, very fragrant and delicious, and distributed to the officials. The emperor thought about its beauty, and the fisherman could not catch it again, knowing it to be a divine and extraordinary creature.

In the second year, the country of Han Tu presented its rare and unusual treasures. The envoy said, "It is seven ten-thousand li from the capital. The birds and beasts can all speak. When chickens and dogs die, they are buried and do not decay. Over many generations, the people of the country wander in the mountains and by the sea, and hear the sounds of chickens and dogs from underground. The owners then dig them up, bring them home, and raise them. Although their feathers and fur may fall out, they grow back over time, becoming smooth and lustrous."

Zhangye Prefecture was home to the flourishing Zhi clan, and thus it was named. Zhi Qi, whose courtesy name was Junzhen, observed all the rituals during his mourning period. His residence was one hundred li from the tomb, and every night when he traveled, he was often accompanied by birds carrying fire, helping him climb mountains and cross rivers, wailing incessantly. He never worried about the dangers, as the night was as bright as day. When he shed tears on stones, they left marks; when he shed tears on withered wood and dead grass, they must all flourish again. When his tears soaked the ground, it became salty, and the locals called it "Salty Village." During the reign of Emperor Zhao, he was praised for his filial piety, and the town was inscribed and commemorated as "Filial Piety Village," with seasonal sacrifices performed and a temple established there. The record states: The heart's path, wherever it goes, penetrates even the most hidden places; truth lies in the subtle, and the obscure becomes clear. The dark light returns the sword of Luyang, and the severe frost stirs the sigh of an ordinary man. Even in the realm of common people, divine traces are still evident. How much more so when one seeks the essence of the spirit, to unite with the fervent heart, making even wood and stone respond mysteriously, and birds and beasts gather in harmony. Wei Yuan's mournful cries caused spring flowers to change their leaves; Shu Tong's early morning rise caused the morning stream to suddenly form a horizontal stone; Xin Shian's traces are seen in the roosting phoenix, and Wei Nong shows virtue in the dream tiger. The actions of the Zhi family are similar to this way. According to the reign of Emperor Zhao of Han, a yellow crane descended into Taiye Pool; today it is called Lin Pool, as it is one body of water with two names. During the reign of Emperor Xuan, there was the auspicious sign of good grain and black millet. It is not clear if this is due to the achievements of Shen Nong and Hou Ji in planting, or if it is what the prince calls, not what the common people eat. To interpret its name, it is ornamental but not substantial. As for birds and animals, divine trees and strange grasses, to see the strange and speak of it, these are the extraordinary of the ages.

Emperor Cheng of Han enjoyed incognito travel, and he built the "Xiaoyou Palace" by the Taiye Pool, with lacquered columns and black silk curtains, and all furnishings, clothing, and vehicles were predominantly black. He was pleased with the dark environment and disliked the light from lamps and candles. The palace women all wore black clothes, and from Ban Jieyu downwards, they all wore black ribbons, and their ornaments, though as splendid as brocade, were covered with sandalwood gauze. Only when they arrived at the "Xiaoyou Palace" did they light candles. After feasts and entertainments, they performed quiet dances, and their steps were so light that they did not raise dust. He enjoyed evening outings. He built a flying pavilion, one zhang square, like a modern carriage, and selected elite soldiers from the Yu Lin to carry it. On the carriage, he felt it moved swiftly, and he heard a sound like wind and thunder inside, indicating its speed, and it was called the "Yunlei Palace." In the palaces he favored, the ground was covered with felt and silk to avoid the noise of wheels and hooves. Although he was indulged in covert pleasures, the people did not suffer or complain. Whenever he returned from his outings, he distributed valuable clothes and delicacies to the women he favored, and left them by the roadside, and the poor and elderly people all sang "Long live the Emperor." Therefore, during the reigns of Hongjia and Yongshi, the country was prosperous, and weapons were rarely used. Thus, Liu Xiang and Gu Yong pointed out and earnestly advised, and the "Xiaoyou Palace" and the flying pavilion were burned down, and extravagant entertainment was abolished. This is what is meant by following the right path, which leads to smooth and easy progress, like a wheel turning effortlessly.

The emperor often on the leisure days of autumn, played with Zhao Feiyan in Taiye Pool, using sandalwood to make a boat, valuing it for not sinking. He decorated the bow with mica, and it was also called "Cloud Boat." He also carved a large paulownia wood into a coiled dragon, carved and decorated to look real, to accompany the Cloud Boat. He used purple osmanthus for the rudder and paddle. When watching the Cloud Boat paddle through the water, playing and picking lotus flowers, the emperor always worried about the lightness and swaying, which might startle Fei Yan. He ordered the skilled watermen to use golden chains to tether the Cloud Boat on the waves. Each time a gentle breeze blew, Fei Yan almost seemed to want to follow the wind into the water. The emperor tied a green silk ribbon to Fei Yan's skirt, and they returned when they were tired of playing. Fei Yan was gradually neglected, and she often complained saying, "I am lowly, how can I again participate in the ribbon-skirt play?" Now Taiye Pool still has a wind shelter, the place where the ribbon was tied to Fei Yan's skirt. The record states: The dignity of a ruler is to sit facing the screen in silent reverence. Therefore, one's movements and rest have their limits, and one's actions and stops have their measures. When going out, the Grand Tutor presents the ceremony of mounting the carriage; when entering, the Junior Tutor recommends the ceremony of ascending the hall. Flags and banners are set up around the gates for protection, and a clean palace is prepared for rest. Emperor Cheng of Han, however, neglected his position as ruler, indulged in minor pleasures, and was easily swayed by tales of immortals. Gu Yong thus boldly remonstrated. Does not the *Book of Documents* say, "Do not disdain small actions, for they will ultimately tarnish great virtue"? This is precisely what it means.

Emperor Ai indulged in luxury and debauchery, frequently promoting flatterers and sycophants. His favored officials competed to present themselves as beautifully adorned and eloquent.
