Dong Xian wore a fine, translucent garment, delicate as a cicada's wing. When the emperor entered the room where he rested, he ordered the attendant to change into light clothing with small sleeves, to show off the use of luxurious sashes and long skirts, making the attire graceful and easy to move. The attendants emulated cutting one's sleeve, and the phrase "cutting the sleeve to avoid waking him up" became a common expression. During the reign of Emperor Ming, Empress Dowager Yin had a dream in which she ate a very delicious melon. The emperor ordered to seek melon seeds from various countries. Dunhuang presented a strange variety of melon seeds, and Mount Heng presented a large peach pit. The melon was named "Qionglong," measuring three feet in length and curved in shape, with a taste sweet as syrup. The elders said, "In the past, a Taoist priest obtained this melon from Mount Penglai, saying it was a divine melon from Mount Kungtung, bearing fruit once every four great eons. The Queen Mother of the West left it in this place, far removed in time, and the fruit still exists." They also said, "The peach blooms under frost and ripens in the heat, and it is said to be food for immortals." The emperor ordered the melon to be planted in the Frosty Forest Garden. The garden was filled with cold fruits, blooming in winter and bearing fruit in the heat, a phenomenon known as "Xiangling," a phonetic variant of "Frosty Forest." The empress dowager remarked, "The peach of the Queen Mother of the West and the melon of the king, though they can be eaten, I shall never plant them here. After my death, the inner attendants saw melon and peach pits in the mirror casket. They looked at them and wept, suspecting they were not of their kind."

Emperor Ai indulged in luxury and sensuality, frequently promoting flatterers and sycophants. Those favored by the emperor competed in adorning themselves with妖丽 (妖艳美丽) and used flattering words to win his favor. Dong Xian wore a single robe made of gossamer-like silk, light as a cicada’s wing. When the emperor entered the chamber where he rested, he ordered the minister to change into light clothing with small sleeves, demonstrating the use of luxurious sashes and long skirts, so that movement would be easier and more graceful. The palace attendants all imitated the act of cutting off a sleeve. It was also said that the sleeve was cut to avoid waking him during sleep. Later Han Emperor Ming’s concubine Lady Yin dreamed of eating a very delicious melon. The emperor ordered the search for such a fruit among the various kingdoms. At that time, Dunhuang presented a strange melon seed, and Hengshan presented a large peach pit. The melon was named “Qionglong,” three feet long, with a curved shape, and its flavor was sweet like honey. The elders said: “Long ago, a Daoist priest obtained this melon from Mount Penglai, claiming it to be the sacred melon of Mount Kungtung, which bears fruit once every four eons. It was left behind by the Queen Mother of Heaven in this place, and though the ages have long passed, its fruit still occasionally appears.” They also said: “The great peach blooms under frost and ripens only in the height of summer, and is also said to be the food of immortals.” The emperor ordered it to be planted in the Shuanglin Garden. The garden was filled with cold-resistant fruits, and during the season of accumulated ice, all fruits were in full bloom. The people called this period “xiāng líng,” a phonetic variation of “shuāng lín,” the name of the garden. Later, Lady Yin said: “The peach of the Queen Mother of Heaven and the melon of the kings—can they be eaten? If so, I shall live forever. How can I then plant them?” After her death, the palace attendants found the seeds of the melon and peach in her mirror case, and upon seeing them, they wept, suspecting they were not of this world.

In the first year of Yongning during the reign of Emperor Zhang, the country of Tiaozhi came to offer unusual auspicious signs. There was a bird called Zhi Que, standing seven feet tall and able to understand human language. When the country was at peace, the Zhi Que birds would fly in groups. In the time of Emperor Wu of Han, the four barbarian tribes submitted, and there was a tame magpie offered. If there was a joyful event, it would flap its wings and fly and cry. According to Zhuangzi, the "Qiao Ling magpie," it is of the same kind. The *Huainanzi* says, "The magpie knows human joy." What is recorded now, though different in size and distance, is the same in essence, so I will briefly mention it. Emperor An enjoyed secret travels, in the outskirts or slept in the open air, setting up tents made of brocade, felt, and embroidered fabrics. By the third year of Yongchu, the state treasury was insufficient, and he ordered officials and commoners to contribute money to become officials. There was Wang Pu of Langya, the descendant of Wang Ji. Wang Ji was first the Zhongwei of Changyi. Wang Pu's family declined for generations. By the time of Emperor An, he was poor and could not become an official. He then carried bamboo slips and a brush, working as a copyist in the market of Luoyang. He was handsome in appearance and had many literary skills. Those who hired him to copy books, men gave him clothes, and women gave him jewels. In one day, he returned with a carriage full of clothes and treasures. He stored grain in granaries, and his nine clans and relatives all depended on his food and clothing. In Luoyang, he was called a good copyist and became wealthy. Wang Pu, when he was poor, dug a well and found an iron seal. The inscription said, "Through labor, one gains wealth, with money reaching the billions. One earth, three fields, military gate clerk." Later, he paid one billion coins to the government and became the Commander of the Central Camp. Three fields and one earth, the character for "camp." The Commander of the Central Camp oversees the gates of the northern army camp, hence the title "military gate clerk." Accumulated virtue brings blessings, and the spirits repay him.

During the third year of the Chuping era of Emperor Ling, he strolled in the Western Garden. He built a thousand rooms for nude bathing, collected green moss to cover the steps, drew canal water to encircle the steps, and the water circulated clearly. He rode boats to float around, had palace attendants ride on them, selected those with fair skin and slender bodies, to hold paddles and oars, and sway and float in the canal. The water was clear, and during the hot summer, he made the boats capsize to see the fair skin of the palace attendants. He also played the song "Inviting Coolness" to bring cool air. The song says: "Cool winds rise, and the sun shines on the canal; green lotus leaves curl during the day and spread at night. Only days are insufficient, but joy is abundant. Clear strings and flowing pipes sing the jade duck; for a thousand years and ten thousand ages, joy is hard to surpass." Lotus plants grew in the canal, as large as umbrellas, one zhang long, offered by the southern regions. Their leaves spread at night and curled during the day, with one stem having four lotus flowers growing together, called "Night-Opening Lotus." Also said to open when the moon rises, thus called "Moon-Opening Lotus."

During the hot summer, the emperor sought refuge in the Naked Play Hall, where he indulged in drinking and feasting throughout the night. The emperor lamented, "If one could live like this forever, then one would be an immortal." Palace women aged fourteen or older and eighteen or younger were all adorned with makeup, removed their outer garments, and wore only their underclothes, sometimes bathing naked together. A fragrant herb from the Western Regions, known as yinchi, was boiled into a bathwater. After bathing and washing, the remaining liquid was poured into a channel, which was named the "Fragrant Flowing Channel." The emperor also ordered the eunuchs to make the sound of a donkey. To the north of the hall, a Rooster-Crowing Hall was constructed, where many chickens were kept. Whenever the emperor was drunk and confused by the dawn, the palace attendants would compete in making the sound of a rooster, confusing the real sound. Then, torches were thrown in front of the hall, startling the emperor awake. When Dong Zhuo destroyed the capital, he dispersed the emperor's concubines and burned the imperial palaces and halls. By the year 265 AD, the place where the torches had been thrown still glowed with light like stars every night. People believed this was divine light and built a small shrine there, naming it the "Shrine of the Remaining Light" to pray for blessings. By the end of Emperor Ming's reign in the Wei dynasty, the shrine was gradually cleared away.

The record states: "Emperor Ming and Emperor Zhang, inheriting the great achievements of their predecessors, extended their influence to all corners of the world and exercised their authority in all directions. Their influence reached remote regions with different customs, and auspicious signs converged like spokes of a wheel. However, Emperor An and Emperor Ling, both engaged in moral corruption. Indeed, things that delight the senses and satisfy the heart rarely do not lead one to be submerged in desires. Without a far-sighted view of rise and fall, who could change or elevate the lower classes? Talent guided by personal desires, deeply engrossed in desires, blocking advice and favoring evil, being lost in debauchery. Just as in past dynasties, no ruler who relied on tyranny and extravagance failed to lose his kingdom and destroy his ancestral rites. When consulting historical records and examining past accounts, it is often found that rulers who were obsessed with dogs and horses, and fond of dragons and cranes, were not the kind of wise kings whose deeds should be passed down to posterity. Internally, they indulged in cruel and excessive pleasures, and externally, they pursued frivolous and wasteful pursuits, delighting the senses and being condemned by future generations. Therefore, the female demon warned of disaster, and Emperor Ling brought ruin to his family through the actions of a notorious figure. The shameful acts of King Jie, who indulged in excessive drinking and naked dancing, and the deceptive actions of King You, who used fire signals to amuse his concubine, all point to the same pattern of moral decline. These events, whether in the Shang or Yan dynasties, are a cause for deep sorrow."

```yaml source_title: "Empress Fu of Emperor Xian of Han" source_author: "Unknown" translator: "Your Name" ``` Empress Fu of Emperor Xian of Han was known for her intelligence, kindness, and wisdom, and she was renowned for her virtue within the palace. When the imperial carriage was defeated by Li Jue and Guo Si, the emperor fled day and night, with the palace attendants in a panic, and there seemed to be no hope of survival. When they reached the river, there were no boats available, so Empress Fu carried the emperor across the river. The river was flowing rapidly, but she felt as if she were being carried by something supernatural, indicating divine assistance. As the soldiers approached the riverbank, she used her body to shield the emperor. The emperor had injured his toe, and Empress Fu wiped the blood with silk and used a jade hairpin to cover the wound, which healed instantly upon application. She washed the emperor's clothes and face with her tears, leaving them as clean and fresh as if they had been washed. The soldiers were deeply moved and impressed, remarking that even in chaos, there was a wise and virtuous woman. Her utmost sincerity and dedication had moved the divine beings.

Empress Fu of Emperor Xian of Han was intelligent, kind, benevolent, and wise, renowned for her virtue within the palace. When the emperor was defeated by Li Jue and his forces, they fled day and night, the palace attendants scattering in panic, with scarcely a chance of survival. When they reached the river, there were no boats, so the empress carried the emperor across. The river ran swiftly, yet she felt as though something beneath her feet was supporting her—evidence of divine assistance. As soldiers drew near the bank, the empress shielded the emperor with her own body. When the emperor was wounded on his foot, the empress wiped the blood with silk, scraped the wound with a jade hairpin, and covered it, and the wound healed instantly. She washed the emperor’s clothes and face with her tears, leaving them as clean as if they had been freshly laundered. The soldiers were moved and awed, remarking that even in chaos, there remained a wise and virtuous woman. Her sincerity was so profound that it moved the unseen spirits of heaven itself.

The Record says: Just as jade and stones can be polished, but their inherent hardness and color cannot be altered, so too can orchids and cassia be broken, but their pure fragrance cannot be hidden. Empress Fu embodied purity and brightness in her appearance and loyalty and integrity in her character. Facing danger, she accepted her fate, a feat that even a brave man could not surpass. Knowing that death was inevitable without regret, she was like Feng Yuan. Such examples are rare throughout history. From the rise of the Han Dynasty to the reigns of Emperor Ai, Emperor Ping, Emperor Yuan, and Emperor Cheng, they still built palaces and gardens, with the Western Capital beginning to become grand and luxurious, followed by the Eastern Capital continuing this trend. They violated the principle of using simple, uncarved beams and deviated from the frugal tradition of the Sacred Pool. Examining the imperial plans and historical records, the extensive descriptions of palaces, fortifications, and cities, since the construction of multi-gate buildings, there has never been such extravagant spending. Emperor Ai expanded the four-season buildings, and Emperor Ling repaired the nude bathing pavilions. If one is deceived by illusions, the gods will resent it; if one is obsessed with craftsmanship, people will suffer. The destruction of nations and families is truly heart-wrenching! As for auspicious signs, rare birds, beautiful flowers, and unique trees, born outside their native soil, with strange colors and sounds, not bound by the rules of the calendar, and not mentioned in historical records, some come due to virtue and achievements, others due to contemporary customs. All these details are fully recorded. Studying all the classics and historical texts, nothing compares to this grandeur.

Guo Kuang, the younger brother of Empress Guo of Emperor Guangwu, accumulated tens of millions in gold, had over four hundred domestic servants, and used gold to make household items. The sound of smelting and forging was so loud that it shook the capital and its suburbs. People said, "De's house, where thunder roars without rain," symbolizing the intense sound of metalworking. In the courtyard, a high pavilion and long corridor were built, and a balance and weights were placed on it to weigh pearls and jade. Under the pavilion, there was a hidden treasure vault, guarded by warriors. The pavilions and terraces were adorned with a mix of treasures, and pearls were hung at the four corners. In the day, they looked like stars; at night, they looked like the moon. Folk saying: "In Luoyang, the De household has more money than anyone else; at night it's like the moon, in the day it's like stars, unmatched in wealth." Those favored ones all used jade vessels to serve food, so in the Eastern Han, the De household was called "jade kitchen and gold cave." Guo Kuang was careful and cautious, and although he lived in wealth and power, he shut the door and lived leisurely, never involved in worldly affairs, to be wise for his time.

The record states: The prosperity of the maternal family, exclusively wielding the power of the inner ruler, all rose to power through favoritism and spread arrogance throughout the world, with evil luck intruding into politics and monopolizing the closeness of the imperial harem. In ancient times, Wei Ran was so wealthy that his riches could tip the balance of the Qin state; in the Han dynasty, Wang Feng and his five brothers were ennobled. Their mansions僭越 in the capital, and beautiful concubines adorned the palace. Precious gifts and southern gold were endlessly enjoyed in the royal treasury, and embroidered brocades and carved decorations adorned buildings. High and spacious halls reached the height of summer houses' splendor, and ornate horses and red carriages exhausted the luxury of vehicles and attire. Since ancient times, no one has been so arrogant as this. Even the examples of Duke Zhuang of Zheng having three wives and Duke Zhao of Lu arranging an eight-row dance in his courtyard are inferior to this. Guo Kuang relied on his marital status for favor and was known for his strictness, gathering wealth from distant places and accumulating the riches of Tao Zhu and Cheng Zheng. Is this enough to describe their prosperity? He did not rely on his family connections or boast about his wealth and power, upholding the virtue of warmth and respect, maintaining the path while holding onto abundance, and being cautious in self-competing. This can be called a divine understanding of the signs.

Liu Xiang, at the end of Emperor Cheng's reign, was cataloging books in the Tielu Pavilion, deeply engrossed in his work. One night, an elderly man appeared, wearing a yellow robe and holding a staff made of green calamus. He ascended the pavilion and approached Liu Xiang, who was sitting alone in the dark, reciting books. The old man then blew on the end of his staff, causing it to emit smoke and light, thereby revealing himself to Liu Xiang. He spoke of the time before the creation of the world. Liu Xiang thus received the text of the "Great Pattern of the Five Elements," and fearing that the extensive discourse might be forgotten, he tore his clothes and sash to record the words. At dawn, the old man departed, and Liu Xiang asked for his name. The old man replied, "I am the essence of Taiyi, and the Heavenly Emperor has heard that the son of the Metal Rabbit has a broad and profound learning, so He descended to observe." He then took out a bamboo slip from his robe, which contained books on astronomy and geography, and said, "I will briefly impart this knowledge to you." When Liu Xiang's son, Liu Xin, came to study from him, Liu Xiang did not recognize the old man as the one who had imparted the knowledge.

Jia Kui, at the age of five, was exceptionally bright and wise. His sister, the wife of Han Yao, who had no children and returned to live with them, was also renowned for her virtue and intelligence. She heard her neighbors reading and would hold Jia Kui against the fence to listen to them. Jia Kui listened quietly without speaking, and his sister was delighted. By the time he was ten years old, he had memorized the Six Classics. His sister said to him, "Our family is poor, and we have never had a teacher come into our home. How could you know about the 'Three Pits' and 'Five Canons' and yet memorize them without missing a word?" Jia Kui replied, "I remember when you held me against the fence to listen to the neighbors read. Now I have not forgotten a single word." He then peeled the bark from the mulberry tree in the courtyard to make writing tablets, sometimes writing on the doors and screens, and would recite and memorize at the same time. After a year, he had mastered all the classics. In the village, whenever there were spectators, they would praise him, saying that his achievements were unparalleled in ancient times. Students came from far and wide to learn from him, some even bringing their children along and staying near his door. He would teach them orally, and those who brought gifts would fill his granaries with grain. It is said that Jia Kui did not earn his living through hard labor but through the cultivation of his tongue, which is what the world calls "tongue cultivation."

Heo Hiu, reticent yet wise, thoroughly memorized the "Three Caves," "Five Classics," yin-yang and numerology, the "River and Luo River" prophecies, ancient proverbs, and historical records from past dynasties. When his disciples asked him questions, he would write notes to answer them, but he was not good at explaining them verbally. He wrote the *Leftist Commentary on Gao Huang* (《左氏膏肓》), the *Gongyang Commentary on Wasted Diseases* (《公羊废疾》), and the *Guliang Commentary on Inflexibility* (《谷梁墨守》), and called them the "Three Deficiencies." The principles he discussed were profound and subtle, and could only be understood by those who knew the timing and could hide the past. When Zheng Kangcheng rose up to attack him, those who sought learning would travel thousands of miles, bringing provisions with them, like small streams flowing into the ocean. The capital city referred to Zheng Kangcheng as the "Spirit of the Classics" and Heo Hiu as the "Sea of Learning." At the age of fourteen, Ren Mo studied without a fixed teacher, carrying his books far into dangerous places. He often said, "If a person does not study, how can they succeed?" Sometimes, he would take shelter under trees, weave reeds into a simple hut, carve brushes from thorny branches, and use tree sap as ink. At night, he would study by the light of stars and the moon, and in the dark, he would light fireflies or wild herbs to illuminate himself. When reading something that resonated with him, he would write it on his clothes to remember it. His disciples admired his diligence and gave him clean clothes to replace the ones he had written on. He would not read anything that was not from a sage. On his deathbed, he said, "A person who loves learning, even if they die, still lives on; a person who does not study, even if they live, is like a walking corpse!" The profound mysteries of the "River and Luo River" (河图洛书), which were not recorded in official texts, he wrote all of these on pillars, walls, and trees in gardens. Those who were eager to learn would come and copy them. At the time, people referred to Ren Mo as the "Garden of Classics."

Cao Zeng, a native of Lu, was originally named Ping. Admiring the conduct of Zeng Shen, he changed his name to Zeng. With immense wealth, he served his parents with utmost propriety, daily offering three sacrificial animals, never lacking in any way. He would not eat new flavors before his parents. When he encountered new flavors while visiting others, he would carry them back to share with his parents. He did not keep chickens or dogs, saying that their noise would disturb his elderly parents. During a severe drought, wells and ponds all dried up. His mother longed for sweet, clear water. Zeng knelt and held a jar, and sweet, clear water gushed forth, more refreshing than usual. He provided food for all poor students. He corrected all the famous books from ancient times, where texts had been lost or corrupted, amassing over ten thousand volumes. After the national crisis was resolved, books from all over the country were collected at Zeng's home, transported in a continuous line of vehicles to the royal palace. His disciples built a shrine outside his home, calling it the "Cao Shifu Shrine." During times of chaos, when every household burned down, Zeng feared that ancient texts would be lost. He built a stone warehouse to store books, hence the Cao family was called the "Book Warehouse." The commentator says: Observing Liu Xiang's fame during the reign of Emperor Cheng of Han, his talent encompassed three ancient periods, his arts included nine sages, since the time of the sun and moon, few have been like him. By the Later Han period, the studies of Jia, He, Ren, and Cao were all considered divine, spanning from ancient times to the present, and there have been none greater. If they were like Yan Hui, nearly perfect; Guan Mei and Zhang Ba, how could they compare to great scholars! As for the five gentlemen, there has never been anyone like them outside Confucius' school; compared to them, others would feel ashamed. Jia's sister, known as a wise woman, could discern the sacred.

拾遗记卷七 魏 文帝所爱美人，姓薛名灵芸，常山人也。父名邺，为酇乡亭长，母陈氏，随邺舍于亭傍。居生穷贱，至夜，每聚邻妇夜绩，以麻蒿自照。灵芸年至十五，容貌绝世，邻中少年夜来窃窥，终不得见。咸熙元夫，谷习出守常山郡，闻亭长有美女而家甚贫。时文帝选良家子女，以入六宫。习以千金宝赂聘之，既得，乃以献文帝。灵芸闻别父母，歔欷累日，泪下沾衣。至升车就路之时，以玉唾壶承泪，壶则红色。既发常山，及至京师，壶中泪凝如血。帝以文车十乘迎之，车皆镂金为轮辋，丹画其毂，轭前有杂宝为龙凤，衔百子铃，锵锵和鸣，响于林野。驾青色之牛，日行三百里。此牛尸屠国所献，足如马蹄也。道侧烧石叶之香，此石重迭，状如云母，其光气辟恶厉之疾。此香腹题国所进也。 拾遗记卷七 In the state of Wei, The beloved beauty of Emperor Wen, surnamed Xue and given name Lingyun, was from Changshan. Her father was named Ye and served as a village headman in Zanxiang, while her mother Chen followed him and lived beside the village hall. They lived in poverty, and at night, they would gather with other women to spin thread by the light of hemp and wormwood. When Lingyun turned fifteen, she was of unparalleled beauty, and the young men in the village would secretly peek at her at night but never saw her face. In the first year of the Shanxi era, Gu Xi was appointed to govern Changshan Prefecture and heard that the village headman had a beautiful daughter from a poor family. At that time, Emperor Wen was selecting virtuous daughters from good families to enter the six palaces. Xi offered a thousand gold pieces as a bribe to win her hand. Once he obtained her, he presented her to Emperor Wen. Upon hearing she was to leave her parents, Lingyun wept for several days, her tears wetting her clothes. When she boarded the carriage and set out on the road, she used a jade spitting pot to catch her tears, which turned the pot red. After leaving Changshan and arriving in the capital, the tears in the pot had congealed like blood. The emperor sent ten ornate carriages to welcome her, each with gold inlaid on the rims of the wheels, painted red on the hubs, and adorned with various treasures forming dragons and phoenixes on the yokes, with a hundred children's bells that rang in harmony, echoing through the forests and fields. She was driven by blue oxen that could travel three hundred miles a day. These oxen were offered by the Shitu Kingdom and had feet like horse hooves. Along the road, the fragrant stone leaves were burned, these stones being layered and resembling mica, their light and fragrance warding off evil and harmful diseases. This incense was presented by the Futi Kingdom.

Ling Yun had not yet reached the capital city by several tens of miles when the glow of the candles continued without interruption, the road was choked with vehicles, and dust rose to obscure the stars and moon, leading people at the time to call it "Dust Night." They also built a platform out of earth, with a base thirty zhang high, and arranged candles at its base, naming it the "Candle Platform," which from a distance appeared like falling stars. Additionally, they placed copper markers every li along the main road, each five chi high, to mark the distance. Therefore, travelers sang: "Green willows line the road, heavy with dust; the dragon-topped and phoenix-roofed palace looms majestically. Breezes and drizzle carry fragrances, and from the earth emerges gold, lighting the platform." These seven words are considered ominous. Placing copper markers to mark the distance on the roadside symbolizes the emergence of gold from the earth. Arranging candles at the base of the platform signifies the fire being beneath the earth. The Han dynasty ruled with the element of fire, while the Wei dynasty ruled with the element of earth. When fire is subdued and earth rises, and gold emerges from the earth, it symbolizes the fall of the Wei and the rise of the Jin. Ling Yun had not yet reached the capital city by ten li when the emperor rode in a luxurious carriage of jade and ivory, observing the grand procession, and sighed, saying, "In the past, it was said, 'morning as a wandering cloud, evening as a passing rain,' but now it is neither cloud nor rain, neither morning nor evening." He changed Ling Yun's name to "Night Come" and, after she entered the palace, she became greatly favored. A foreign envoy presented a hairpin adorned with a pearl and a dragon and phoenix. The emperor said, "Even precious gems like pearls and emeralds cannot surpass it, let alone the weight of a dragon and phoenix!" He then declined to accept it. Night Come excelled in needlework, able to complete her creations in the darkness of the inner chambers without the aid of candlelight. If it was not made by Night Come, the emperor would not wear it. In the palace, she was known as the "Needle Goddess."

The Record states: The reigns of the Five Emperors were marked by cycles of rise and fall, with their ebb and flow evident in speech. During the Spring and Autumn period, children's rhymes were believed to foretell the future, and at the end of the Han dynasty, prophetic sayings were prevalent. Some revelations were made clear through ancient precedents, while others were revealed through symbols or inscriptions. The collective examination of the *Book of the Yellow River* and the *Book of the Luo River* shows that the timing or context of these revelations varies. Emperor Yao, representing the "fire" element, peacefully abdicated in favor of Emperor Shun, and the Han dynasty, claiming the "fire" element, succeeded the Wei dynasty. Historical events and dynastic changes follow a pattern of continuity, and such patterns are appropriate or fitting. The elevation of status through the use of jade is contingent on specific events or circumstances. Those with gentle or delicate qualities are promoted, and those skilled in subtle or refined arts are favored. Charm and elegance are not enduring, lasting only for a single generation, and the removal of doubt or suspicion extends the luxury and nobility of such a state.

Emperor Ming of Wei built the Cloud-Piercing Platform, personally digging the earth, and all the ministers carried shovels. The weather was overcast, freezing, and cold, and the dead were lying one upon another. Bronze tripods in Luoyang and Ye moved on their own during the night. There were also sounds of lamentation heard underground in the palace. Gao Tanglong and others submitted a memorial advising, "The ruler should be calm to nurture the people. Now, the lamentation is heard by both people and spirits. We hope to reduce excessive luxury and expenses to promote frugality." The emperor still did not stop, seeking rare and extraordinary items, and collecting valuable treasures. He built pavilions and terraces for many years until completion. There were many warnings, and the emperor eventually eliminated extravagance and returned to simplicity, collecting and burying the dead. People and spirits responded, and all auspicious signs appeared. In Mount Tai, there was a stone with intertwined patterns, twelve zhang tall, looking like a pine tree, with patterns that stood out, as if carved by humans. From bottom to top, it was connected, but in the middle, it opened up to six feet wide, appearing like a true tree. The elders said, "During the end of the Qin dynasty, the two stones were over a hundred steps apart, overgrown and without a path. When the Wei dynasty began, they gradually came closer, like a pair of gateways." The stone is of the earth element, and the Wei dynasty is of the earth element, making this a divine sign. Gardens and the homes of the people had grass and trees that grew together. There was a plant called the "divine grass," resembling the herb *shizi*, with one stem and a hundred branches. During the day, the branches spread out, and at night, they came together into one stem, never missing one, called the "divine grass." In Pei, a yellow dragon appeared in the region of west and south, which are associated with the earth element. The emperor then built altars for the directions of west and south, with yellow stars shining at night. He also built pavilions for the stars of Shen and Bi, and worshiped these stars. In the Hui region, rituals were performed regularly according to the seasons.

Ren Cheng Wang Chang, the son of Emperor Wu, was young and resolute. He studied the techniques of divination and astrology, and memorized thousands of words from the Six Classics and the *Hong Fan*. When Emperor Wu sought advice on the most advantageous way to wage war against Wu and Shu, Chang provided his counsel. Chang was skilled in both archery and swordsmanship, and could hit a target from a hundred paces, even hitting the hair on the target's face. At one point, the people of Lelang presented a tiger with fur patterned like a brocade. The tiger was placed in an iron cage, and the people of the Yin dynasty, so terrified, dared not look at it. Chang dragged the tiger's tail around his arm, and the tiger remained silent and docile. None could fail to admire his divine courage. At another time, a white elephant calf from Nanyue was presented before the emperor, and Chang struck its nose with his hand, causing the elephant to kneel and remain motionless. Emperor Wen cast a bell weighing ten thousand catties and placed it in the Chonghua Hall. When the emperor wished to move it, a hundred strong men could not budge it, but Chang lifted it and carried it away. All over the world, hearing of his divine courage, everyone put down their weapons and fortified themselves. Emperor Wen remarked, "With the king's heroic prowess, conquering Ba and Shu would be like a kite carrying away a dead mouse!" Chang passed away, and his funeral rites were conducted according to the customs of the Han dynasty's Prince of Dongping. As the funeral procession passed, people heard the sounds of hundreds of people weeping in the air. The participants remarked that the soldiers who had died in battle without proper burial were now at peace, and the spirits knew gratitude, so people praised the king's virtue. The official historian wrote a three-volume account of Ren Cheng Wang Chang's deeds, which was later stored in the imperial library.

In the third year of the Jian'an era (208 AD), the kingdom of Xutu offered a red stone chicken. The creature's color was like cinnabar, and its size resembled that of a swallow. It called at the appropriate time, and its voice could be heard far away. When the kingdom heard its call, they would sacrifice animals to honor it. The creature was found by digging at the place where it called. If the world was at peace, it would fly gracefully and in a balanced manner, considered a good omen. It was also known as the "Golden Rooster." The kingdom had no chickens and relied on the creature to determine the time by listening to its calls from underground. Daoists said: "In ancient times, the immortal Tong Jun collected stones, entering a cave for several miles, and found the red stone chicken. He ground it into a medicine, and those who consumed it gained vitality and longevity." During the reign of Emperor Wu of Han in the first year of the Baoding era (113 AD), the Western regions offered rare and exotic gifts, including a tiger amber swallow. Placing it in a quiet room, it called and flew within the room, similar to the red stone chicken. The *Luo Shu* (River Map) states: "The imperial emblem, the virtue of earth, and the good omen for the Wei dynasty."

In the second year of Emperor Ming's reign, he established the Spirit Bird Garden, where strange birds and unusual beasts from distant lands were housed. The Kingdom of Kunming offered the Sputtering Gold Bird. The people of Kunming said, "This bird originates from a place nine thousand li away from the Burning Isle. It resembles a sparrow in shape but is yellow in color, with soft and dense feathers. It often soars over the sea. Whoever captures it considers it an extremely auspicious omen. Hearing of the virtue of the Great Wei, its influence reaches the distant and remote, so they crossed mountains and seas to offer it to the great state." The emperor obtained this bird and kept it in the Spirit Bird Garden, feeding it with pearls and giving it turtle brain to drink. The bird often spat out gold dust like millet, which could be cast into vessels. In the time of Emperor Wu of Han, someone offered a divine sparrow, which was of this kind. This bird is afraid of frost and snow, so a small house was built to house it, named the "Frost-Repelling Pavilion," with all windows and doors made of water crystal to allow light to pass through inside and out. The palace ladies competed to use the gold the bird spat out to adorn their hairpins and ornaments, calling it "Frost-Repelling Gold." Thus, the palace ladies would mock each other, saying, "If you don't wear Frost-Repelling Gold, how can you win the emperor's favor?" Those who flattered and seduced competed chaotically to use this precious gold for their adornment, and carried it with them in their travels and sleep to seek favor. When the Wei dynasty perished, the ponds and terraces were reduced to ashes, and the Sputtering Gold Bird also flew away on its own.

In the second year of Xianxi (265-266 AD), there was an unusual creature in the palace at night, white and clean, moving around the palace. The eunuchs saw it and reported to the emperor. The emperor said, "The palace is secluded and secret; if there are unusual creatures, they are not auspicious." He ordered the eunuchs to watch for the creature. Indeed, they saw a white tiger cub running through the rooms. The guards threw a spear at it, which hit its left eye. When they went to check, only blood was found on the ground, and the tiger was no longer visible. They searched the entire palace and all pools and wells but found nothing. They then checked the treasure house and found a jade tiger-head pillow with a wounded eye and wet blood stains. The emperor, knowledgeable in ancient matters, said, "During the Han Dynasty, after the execution of Liang Ji, a jade tiger-head pillow was found, said to be a gift from the Danchi Kingdom. Upon checking under the chin, there were seal script characters. It was said to be Emperor Xin's pillow, which he used with Lady Daji. It is a relic from the Shang Dynasty." According to the *Five Emperors' Annals*, Emperor Xin was the last ruler of the Shang Dynasty. Up to the time of Xianxi, many years have passed, and the pillow has been passed down through generations. Valuable treasures, if kept for a long time, can develop spirits, and it must be a divine entity possessing it.

During the year when the state of Wei ceded power to the state of Jin, a white light appeared near the northern gate of the imperial city, resembling birds, and would occasionally fly around. Officials reported this phenomenon to the emperor. Upon investigation, they found a white swallow, which was considered a divine object. They then made a golden cage for it and placed it in the palace. After ten days, the swallow disappeared. Some scholars commented, "This is an auspicious sign of the metal element, associated with the state of Jin. In ancient times, during the reign of Shikuang, a white swallow came to nest." Checking the *Book of Auspicious Signs* confirmed this interpretation. The white color is associated with the metal element, and Shikuang was a person from the state of Jin, so the ancient and modern interpretations align. Xue Xia, a man from Tianshui, was exceptionally learned. His mother dreamed that someone gave her a box of clothes and said, "This woman will give birth to a wise and virtuous son, honored by emperors." The mother remembered the date of the dream. When Xue Xia was born, he reached adulthood and demonstrated extraordinary talent and eloquence. Emperor Wen of Wei discussed with him all day long, and his responses were fluent and without hesitation. The emperor said, "In the past, Gong Sun Long was known for his sharp debating skills, but he was often considered eccentric and unreliable. Now, what you say is only about the words of the sages. You are as good as Ziyu and Zixia, two famous disciples of Confucius, and even if Confucius were in Wei, he would consider you a top student again." The emperor wrote a letter to Xue Xia, calling him "the top student," and he held the position of Secretary of the Secretariat. Despite his poverty, the emperor gave him a robe, fulfilling the original dream. Xue Xia was the most famous of his time and a great scholar.

Tian Chou, a native of Beiping, was deeply grieved and yearned for Liu Yu after his death. He went to Liu Yu's tomb and offered a simple meal of chicken and wine. The sound of his sorrowful cries echoed through the forest and fields, causing the birds flying overhead to mourn and the wild animals to lie down and cry out. Tian Chou lay in the grass, when suddenly someone appeared and said, "Liu Yu of Youzhou has come, and he wishes to speak with you about matters of his life." With divine insight and far-sighted wisdom, Tian Chou knew it was the spirit of Liu Yu. As the spirit approached, Tian Chou bowed, unable to control his tears, and they shared the meal of chicken and wine together. Tian Chou became drunk, and Liu Yu said, "Gongsun Zan is searching for you urgently; you should hide to avoid danger!" Tian Chou bowed and replied, "I have heard of the duty of loyalty between a ruler and his subject. In life, one should fulfill all rites. Now that I see your spirit, I wish to return to the underworld with you, where we will both remain immortal. How can I escape?" Liu Yu said, "You are a gentleman of unwavering integrity throughout the ages. Be cautious and maintain your propriety!" Suddenly, the spirit disappeared, and Tian Chou woke up from his drunken stupor.

Cao Hong, the younger brother of Emperor Wu, was wealthy with extensive property and a herd of fine horses. During Emperor Wu's pursuit of Dong Zhuo, he lost his horse at night, and Cao Hong gave his own horse to the emperor. This horse was named "White Swan." When it ran, one could only hear the wind in the ears, and its hooves seemed not to touch the ground. Upon reaching the Bian River, Cao Hong could not cross, so Emperor Wu pulled him onto the horse and they crossed together, traveling hundreds of miles in an instant. The horse's hooves and hair were not wet. People at the time said it was as if they were riding the wind, and it was also a divine steed of its time. A saying goes: "Leaping into empty space, the White Swan of the Cao family." The record states: The ruler extends his domain to form a state, using the sea and mountains as fortifications, which is indeed for the peace and virtue of the people, ruling with ease. Abandoning the expenses of travel, guiding through the path of education, not building grand palaces, but having simple gardens. Using untrimmed logs for beams, the great Tang dynasty exemplified frugality; living in humble dwellings and eating simple food, Yu the Great warned against luxury. By the time of the three dynasties, this principle was lost. Wasting resources and labor, competing in pride and beauty, the extravagance of jade palaces, the wealth of jade platforms, exhausting the wonders of divine craftsmanship, human labor was arduous. By the time of the Spring and Autumn period, the royal house had declined, city builders sang songs, exhausted by their labor. The Jin dynasty built the Qichui Palace, causing public resentment due to the effort; the Song dynasty's construction of the Zemen Gate caused deep sorrow among the laborers. The accumulation of expenses in Gusu (Suzhou) before, and the exhaustion in A'fang Palace later.

Believing that his legacy was as solid as rivers and mountains and surpassing all generations in fame, the destruction of ancestral worship brought profound sorrow. Observing Emperor Ming, he stepped into the fertile and prosperous center of the land, where his power and influence intimidated even the strongest of past dynasties. Auspicious signs and divine treasures were frequently recorded in history, and exotic and precious tributes arrived regularly without fail. Holding power over three regions and being dominant throughout the world, yet the moral teachings had diminished compared to those of Yao and Yu, and the current dynasty was inferior to the Zhou and Han dynasties. Eastern troubles with Min and Wu, and western problems with Qiong and Shu, led to annual military campaigns and daily expenditure of resources. He was unable to nurture the common people and look ahead to simpler times, and his palaces and gardens were extremely luxurious and expansive. Ultimately, this led to destruction, and it is indeed so. Ren Cheng possessed deep strategy and divine courage, as well as comprehensive wisdom and skills, which even the archery and swordsmanship of Laizhou and Pengmeng could not surpass. Tian Chou treated the dead as if they were alive, maintaining his integrity, and his sincerity and dedication reached the level of divinity. Cao Hong had a heart of loyalty and bravery, caring for his family and the country. These individuals are like the swift horses of Mu Man, compared to the "White Swan," and can be said to be equally capable.

**Lost Records, Volume 8** **Wu** When Sun Jian's mother was pregnant with him, she dreamed of her intestines emerging and wrapping around her waist, and a young girl carried her, circling around the Wu Gate (阊门). The girl then gave her a single stem of fragrant grass. The girl said, "This is a good omen, and you will surely give birth to a heroic son. Now, you are granted land, and you will rule the region of the Wing and the Altar, standing as one of the three major powers. In a hundred years, a unique treasure will be bestowed upon someone." After the girl finished speaking, the mother woke up and sought divination. The diviner said, "The dream of the young girl carrying the mother around the Wu Gate is the essence of Venus, a divine influence that brought the dream." The rise of emperors is always accompanied by divine signs, and the white light is golden. When Wu eventually fell and the Jin dynasty rose, the dream was proven to be a true omen.

The record says: According to *Wu Shu*, it is recorded that when Sun Jian's mother was pregnant with him, she dreamt of intestines emerging and winding around the city gate of Chengyang. This differs from Wang's account. The West is the position of metal, which aligns with the virtue of Jin; the signs of rise and fall manifest later, indicating the fall of Wu and the rise of Jin. The six dreams and eight omens, clearly stated in *Zhouyi*, are similar but not identical to the act of granting a lily and holding a sun. And the year of Wu's rise, the title "Jiacheng," and the verified auspicious reeds are all significant. By the first year of Tai Kang in Jin, Sun Hao sent six golden seals, saying, "There were no jade craftsmen, so gold was used for the seals." The Sun family ruled江东, encompassing the Baiyue, swallowing Hanyang, and instilling fear in the Central Plains, with an industry of wealth and strength comparable to the three great powers. At times there were those not yet subjugated, and wars broke out annually, frequently troubled by Qiong and Shu, and filled with resentment towards Yan and Wei. The four directions were not pacified, and there were frequent military campaigns. Following this with military campaigns, adhering to frugality and simplicity, eliminating extravagant spending, blocking the path of luxury and decay, not wanting to burden the people of the four directions. It was not that there were no jade craftsmen. Indeed, they could consider the hills and pools as light, and the thorns as worthless, Han discarded the dust that filled the carriage, and Yan abandoned the unpolished jade on the hall, sinking to the bottom of the river, the meaning is clear from ancient times, striving for frugality and simplicity, is this not noble? When Wu fell, the six generations of golden seals were returned to Jin, and the dream of Sun Jian's mother was verified.

Lady Zhao of Wu, the younger sister of Chancellor Da, was skilled in painting, unparalleled in her artistry. She could weave clouds, dragons, and serpents with colored silk between her fingers, creating works that ranged from a foot in size to a square inch. In the palace, she was called the "Wondrous Weaver." Sun Quan often lamented the unrest in Wei and Shu, and during the intervals of military campaigns, he wished to find a skilled painter to depict landscapes, terrain, and military formations. Chancellor Da then recommended his sister. Sun Quan asked her to depict the forms of the Five Great Mountains, rivers, seas, cities, and military formations. Lady Zhao said, "The colors of ink and paint are easily faded and cannot be treasured for long; I can embroider on the square pieces of cloth of various states, depicting the forms of the Five Mountains, rivers, seas, cities, and military formations." After completing her work, she presented it to the ruler of Wu, and people at the time called her the "Wondrous Embroiderer." Even the intricate needlework of a monkey on a thorn or a cloud ladder flying with a celestial bird could not surpass the beauty of her embroidery. Sun Quan was in Zhaoyang Palace, feeling the heat, and unveiled the purple gauze curtain. Lady Zhao said, "This is not precious."

During the reign of Sun Quan, he asked his wife to point out her intentions. She replied, "I wish to exhaust my thoughts and consider everything thoroughly, so that the cool breeze can enter on its own through the fine silk curtain. Looking outside, there is no obstruction, and those in attendance feel cool and light, as if riding the wind." Sun Quan praised her. The wife then took a lock of her hair and used divine glue to join it. The divine glue comes from the country of Yuyi and can join broken bowstrings, even a hundred times. She then wove it into a fine fabric, which took many months to complete. She cut it into a curtain, and when viewed from inside and outside, it moved lightly like smoke. The room inside became cool. At that time, Sun Quan was often on military campaigns, and he always carried this curtain with him, using it as a tent during campaigns. When unfolded, it was one zhang wide; when rolled up, it could fit in a pillow. People at the time called it "silk thread." Thus, Wu had the "Three Wonders," and no one in the world could match its excellence. Later, there were those who sought favor by flattering, saying the wife was using magic to dazzle the ruler, as a result, she was demoted. Even though she was suspected and demoted, her skill was still recorded. When Wu fell, her whereabouts are unknown.

Lady Pan of Wu, whose father had been punished by law, was sent to a weaving room where she was imprisoned. Her beauty was unparalleled, making her the most beautiful woman in the east of the river (Yangtze). More than a hundred other women were imprisoned with her, and they revered her as a goddess, treating her with respect but keeping their distance. The officials reported her to the ruler of Wu, who ordered her portrait to be drawn. Lady Pan was so distressed that she stopped eating, becoming thinner and changing her appearance. The artists drew her true likeness and presented it to the ruler, who was pleased upon seeing it. He touched a jade如意 (a ceremonial object) with his hand, and it broke. He exclaimed, "She is a goddess, even her sad appearance can bewitch people, how much more so when she is happy!" He then ordered a carriage to fetch her from the weaving room and brought her into the harem, where she was indeed favored for her beauty. Whenever he went to the昭宣台 (a scenic place) with her, he was very pleased, and after drinking to intoxication, he spat into a jade pot. He ordered a servant to pour the contents at the base of the platform, where he found a fire gem ring. He hung it on a pomegranate branch and built a platform there, naming it the Ring Pomegranate Platform. At that time, someone advised him, "Now Wu and Shu are competing for dominance, the name 'Return Liu' will bring disaster!" Sun Quan then changed the name to Pomegranate Ring Platform. He also went fishing with her on the fishing platform and caught a big fish. The king was very happy, and Lady Pan said, "I have heard that fish cry, now they are happy, but happiness will bring sorrow, so be deeply cautious!" By the end of his reign, he gradually fell out of favor and was eventually dismissed. People at the time said, "The lady was divinely perceptive." The ruler of Wu then disbanded the banquet, and the lady was indeed dismissed and banished. The base of the fishing platform still exists today.

The Record says: The two noblewomen, Zhao and Pan, were renowned for their beauty and skill, charming and almost divine in their grace. They were indeed comparable to the beautiful women of the Han dynasty who accompanied the emperor on his travels, and to the romantic and mystical elements of the Chu king and the goddess Xiang. Yet, they were able to avoid the favor of those who were untrustworthy and recognize the right moments for advancement or retreat. Fullness inevitably leads to loss, principles rise and fall, and prosperity is followed by decline; this is a clear truth. When discussing the rise and fall of fortunes, it is like the natural cycles of plants: the blooming and withering, the stretching and relaxing, are the inevitable trends of nature. Flattery and insincere praise, which even wise rulers can be deceived by, are common. Therefore, the virtuous conduct of Shen and Bao in the Zhou dynasty is recorded, and the notable figures Ban and Zhao in the Han dynasty are detailed. Different generations share similar experiences, and this is indeed a cause for reflection! In the first year of the Huanglong era, the capital was established in Wuchang. At that time, from the south of Yuezhi, a mythical bird called the "back to light" bird was presented. It resembled a crane, did not face the light, and always oriented its nest towards the north. It had more flesh and less feathers, and could make many different sounds. When it heard the sounds of bells, chimes, flutes, and pipes, it would flap its wings and shake its head. At the time, people considered it a good omen. That year, the capital was moved to Jianye, and exotic gifts were received from distant lands. The people of Wu, known for their unique dialect, mispronounced "back to light" as "back to death." The bird was considered a great omen of evil, and it was predicted that within a century, there would be significant turmoil, betrayal, and destruction. People would flee, and cities would become desolate, with no signs of life. Indeed, as predicted, the bird disappeared thereafter.

Zhang Cheng's mother, Sun, was traveling by a small boat on a river when she was pregnant with him. Suddenly, a white snake three feet long leaped into the boat. The mother prayed, "If this is a sign of good fortune, do not harm me!" She wrapped the snake and brought it back, placing it in the room. After one night, the snake was no longer seen, and she lamented its loss. The neighbors talked to each other, saying, "Yesterday, we saw a white crane with raised wings flying into the clouds at Zhang's house." They told this to Zhang Cheng's mother, who asked a diviner to interpret the sign. The diviner said, "This is a sign of good fortune. Snakes and cranes are symbols of longevity. From the room to the clouds, it symbolizes rising from low to high. In ancient times, King Helu of Wu buried his sister, accompanied by beautiful women, precious treasures, and unusual swords, exhausting the wealth of southern Jiang. Before ten years had passed, carved clouds covered the ravines, beautiful women roamed above the tomb, white cranes flew in the forest, and white tigers roared on the mountainside. All were spirits of the past, now appearing in the world. This will make your descendants surpass the highest ranks of officials and gain fame in the Jiang region. If you have a son, you can name him Baihu (White Crane)." When Zhang Cheng was born, he became Chancellor and General of Wu, living past ninety years old, fulfilling the omens of the snake and the crane.

The Record says: When a state is about to fall, its signs are seen beforehand. The *Classic* states: "The divine sees it, observing its virtue." When one surrenders with bound hands, this is the effect. Snakes and cranes, the most intelligent among animals, are considered auspicious signs by the Zhang clan. According to *The Spring and Autumn Annals of Wu and Yue* and various sayings, King Helu of Wu, with his elaborate and extravagant burials, buried live concubines and hoarded many treasures. Hundreds of years later, a spirit crane soars in the forest and valleys, and a divine tiger roars on the hills. The sword Zhanlu flew into the state of Chu. The accumulation of spirits and strange phenomena, reaching the height of opulence, yet being strange and out of balance, is not as good as decaying quickly. In the past, Duke Huan of Song and Lady Sheng were criticized by historians for their arrogance and confusion, while the descendants of Ying Bo and Yang Sun were praised by the virtuous for their adherence to proper rites. Observing ancient times, pointing to detailed accounts of the middle ages, seeking through historical records, the difference between frugality and extravagance is vast. As for the late period of a dynasty, practices become increasingly showy and artificial. Excessive indulgence in life and the practice of human sacrifice in death, accumulating precious treasures, only to be buried in dust and forgotten, being separated in death, how sad it is! Lu Meng entered Wu, and the ruler of Wu encouraged his studies. Lu Meng then extensively read many books, taking the *I Ching* as his main focus. He often drank to excess at Sun Ce's table, suddenly fell asleep, and in his dream recited the entire *I Ching*. He suddenly woke up. Everyone asked him about it. Lu Meng said: "I dreamed of Fu Xi, Duke of Zhou, and King Wen, who discussed with me the rise and fall of the world and the principles of the sun and moon, none of which were not thoroughly profound and exquisite. Not fully grasping the profound meaning, I merely recited the text." Everyone present said: "Lu Meng's mutterings were understood as the *I Ching*."

The Record states: When sincerity reaches its utmost, it harmonizes with the unseen, matching the brightness of the sun and moon, aligning with the cycles of the four seasons, thus able to converge virtue with the wisdom of the past and align the way with divine subtlety. If Zheng was inspired by a sage, and Zhou Pan had a prophetic dream in Dongli, their actions were the same but the circumstances differed, their glory extending far and wide. They explored the obscure and delved into the profound, grasping the subtle meanings. The various teachings of Confucius' school are not as profound as the learning of Master Lu. Sun He was fond of Deng, often placing her on his knees. Under the moonlight, he danced with a jade如意, accidentally injuring her cheek, causing blood to flow and stain her clothes, making her more distressed. She licked her wound herself and ordered the imperial physician to prepare medicine. The physician said, "If you can get the marrow of a white otter, mixed with jade and amber powder, it will remove the mark." He immediately offered a hundred gold pieces as a reward for anyone who could obtain the otter's marrow. A fisherman from Fuchun said, "This creature knows when someone wants to take it and hides in a stone cave. Wait until the time when the otter is sacrificed, and if one dies in a fight, there should be dry bones in the cave. Even without marrow, the bones can be ground into powder with jade and sprinkled on the wound, which will remove the mark." Sun He then ordered the preparation of this ointment, but there was too much amber, and when it was applied, it left red spots like cinnabar. When viewed closely, it became even more beautiful. Various concubines wanted to gain favor and all used cinnabar to paint their cheeks before gaining his attention. They influenced each other in a seductive way, leading to a decadent custom.

Emperor Sun Liang created a translucent screen, which was very thin and clear. He often unfolded it during clear moonlit nights. He had four favorite concubines, all of unparalleled beauty from ancient times: one named Chao Shu, another named Li Ju, the third named Luo Zhen, and the fourth named Jie Hua. He made four perfumes for them, each from exotic and rare ingredients. These perfumes retained their fragrance even after many washes, and thus were called "hundred-wash perfumes." Sometimes, people named the perfumes after the women, so there were Chao Shu Perfume, Li Ju Perfume, Luo Zhen Perfume, and Jie Hua Perfume. Whenever Emperor Sun Liang went out, these four women accompanied him, always in the order of their perfumes' names, without ever changing. The room where they stayed was called "Thoughtful Fragrant Boudoir," a place designed for romantic and intimate activities.

**Shu** Gansu, the wife of Liu Bei, was born in humble circumstances. A village diviner once said, "This girl will be honored, reaching the highest position in the palace." When Gansu grew up, her appearance was particularly unusual, and at the age of eighteen, her skin was as soft and smooth as jade, and her demeanor was charming and beautiful. Liu Bei summoned her into a silk tent, and those who looked at her from outside saw her as if she were a gathering of snow under the moon. A jade figure, three feet tall, was presented from Henan, and Liu Bei placed it beside Gansu. During the day, he would discuss military strategies with her, and at night, he would embrace her and play with the jade figure. He often said that jade is precious because its virtue is like that of a gentleman, and how much more so for a human figure that cannot be played with? Gansu and the jade figure were equally white and smooth, and those who saw them were often confused as to which one was which. The favored concubines not only envied Gansu but also the jade figure. Gansu once intended to destroy the jade figure and warned Liu Bei, saying, "In the past, Zi Han did not value jade as a treasure, and the *Spring and Autumn Annals* praised him for this. With Wu and Wei still not defeated, how can one indulge in frivolous playthings? To indulge in such temptations will breed doubt; do not indulge further!" Liu Bei then removed the jade figure, and the favored concubines were all dismissed. At that time, the gentlemen discussed that Gansu was a wise and intelligent woman.

Mí Zhú used the strategies of Tao Zhu, and his wealth increased daily by billions, comparable to that of a royal family, with a thousand treasure houses. Mí Zhú had a nature to provide relief to the living and comfort the dead. In his courtyard, there was an ancient tomb within the stable, containing a corpse. At night, he heard the sound of weeping. Mí Zhú then sought out the source of the weeping and suddenly saw a woman with her back exposed, approaching him. She said, "In the late Han dynasty, I was killed by the Red Eyebrows, and my coffin was stripped. Now I lie exposed on the ground, ashamed to be seen by others during the day, for nearly two hundred years. Now I beg you, General, to bury me deeply and provide me with old clothes to cover my body." Mí Zhú agreed, immediately ordering a coffin to be made and old clothes to be prepared, placing her in the tomb and setting up a sacrifice. After a year, while walking along a winding path, he suddenly saw the same woman again, dressed entirely in blue cloth. She said to Mí Zhú, "Your wealth can support you for a lifetime, but you are destined to suffer from a fire disaster. Now I give you a nine-foot-long piece of green reed as a token of gratitude for your kindness in burying me and providing me with clothes." Mí Zhú took the staff and returned home. In the neighborhood where he lived, people often saw green vapor in the shape of dragons and snakes around his house. Someone asked Mí Zhú, "Is this not a supernatural occurrence?" Mí Zhú then began to suspect this strangeness and asked his servants. They replied, "We often see the green reed staff appearing and disappearing outside the gate, and we suspect it to be a divine object, but we dare not speak of it."

Mi Zhu was a man of a suspicious nature who believed in occult practices. If anyone spoke against him, he would punish them severely, so his servants dared not speak. His wealth was like a mountain, impossible to count. Inside, he used square vessels and bottles to display large pearls as big as eggs, scattered throughout the courtyard, calling it the "Treasure Courtyard," and no one outside could see it. A few days later, dozens of young boys dressed in green suddenly appeared and said, "There will be a fire disaster at Mi Zhu's house, not leaving a single thing intact. Thanks to your compassion and care for the dead, the heavenly way does not wrong your virtue, so we come to ward off this fire. To ensure your wealth is not entirely lost, from now on, you should also be vigilant!" Mi Zhu then dug ditches and channels around his warehouse. Ten days later, a fire broke out in the warehouse, burning a tenth of the pearls and jade. All of it was caused by the sun's reflection and dryness, which naturally burns things. When the fire was at its peak, dozens of young boys dressed in green came to put it out. A green mist like a cloud covered the fire, and it immediately went out. The young boys said, "Collect many cranes and other birds to ward off fires; cranes can gather water in their nests." The family then collected thousands of cranes and kept them in ponds and channels to ward off fires. Mi Zhu sighed, "Human life and wealth have limits; one should not be overly greedy, as it can become a burden and harm oneself." At the time when the three kingdoms were in conflict, military expenses were ten times higher, so he donated his treasures and carriages to assist Lord Liu Bei: one billion pounds of gold, silk and brocade piled up like hills, and ten thousand fine horses. After the fall of Shu, he had nothing left and died with regret.

Zhou Qun was exceptionally skilled in arithmetic and divination. While traveling in Mount Min to gather herbs, he encountered a white ape descending from a peak, facing him and standing there. Zhou Qun drew out the book knife he wore and threw it at the ape. The ape transformed into an old man, who held a jade tablet eight inches long and handed it to Zhou Qun. Zhou Qun asked, "When were you born?" The old man replied, "I am now very old and have forgotten my age and the month, but I still remember the time of the Yellow Emperor, when I began to study the calendar and astronomy. Fenghou and Rongcheng, both historians of the Yellow Emperor, taught me the calendar and astronomy. By the time of Emperor Zhuanxu, the movements of the sun, moon, and stars were more accurately determined, though there were still many differences. During the Spring and Autumn period, there were scholars like Zi Wei, Zi Ye, and Bi Zao, whose strategies were effective but did not fully grasp the essence. In recent generations, the rise and fall of dynasties have been too numerous to remember, and knowledge has been passed down through generations. By the time of the Han dynasty, there was Lu Xiaohong, who partially grasped the essence." Zhou Qun was impressed by his words and became even more diligent in his studies. He then carefully examined the calendar and its cycles, verifying them with the *Tiangui* (divinatory texts), and predicted that the Shu kingdom would fall. The following year, as predicted, the Shu kingdom surrendered to the Wu kingdom. All said, "Zhou Qun is expert in the subtle aspects of yin and yang." The people of Shu called him a "later sage." The strange transformation of the white ape is similar to what was recorded by the people of Yue, though the events are somewhat fantastical and seem to be a mix of truth and fiction.

The Record states: Sun He, Sun Liang, and Liu Bei were all bewitched by indulgence in sensual pleasures, forgetting the strategies of military affairs. Compared to the mighty Wei, they failed to achieve success in conquests, and it is indeed a cause for lament! Zhou Jun's learning reached the level of divine insight, and the omen of the white monkey is similar to the tale of the sword told by the man from Yue. The story is fanciful and far-fetched, though it may not be entirely false. Yin and yang alternate in creation, and the five elements cycle in their use. Water and fire both generate and destroy each other. The *Huainanzi* states, "The sphere of the moon's dew condenses into water," to counteract fire with water. The Mi family was wealthy in rare and precious items, carving the sphere into the shapes of birds and beasts, like the earth dragon used to summon rain. The sound of the *jiangji* bird is confused with the sound of the sphere, likely a distortion of sound. Feathers and the like cannot withstand intense fire, it is contrary to logic and inconsistent with reality. In earlier classics and ancient texts, the story is explained in detail.

The *Shiyiji* (拾遺記) Volume Nine **Jin Dynasty Events** When Emperor Wu of Jin was serving as a high-ranking official, three grass plants suddenly sprouted in the courtyard of his mansion. The grass had golden stems and green leaves, resembling a blend of gold and jade. The flower stems were delicate and weak, looking like lamps. At the time, no one knew what kind of auspicious grass this was, so it was kept hidden from the outside world. There was a Qiang person named Yao Fu, with the courtesy name Shifen, who worked in the stables and took care of the horses. He was skilled in divination and astronomy and said, "This grass is a sign of the Golden Virtue (金德)." Yao Fu lived to be 98 years old, and Yao Xiang was his grandson. Yao Fu loved reading and drinking, and he often spoke about the rise and fall of emperors when he was drunk. He was skilled in humor and wit, and his cleverness was endless. He often said, "The waters of the nine major rivers are not enough to quench the thirst for fermented grains, the wood of the eight marshes is not enough to make firewood, and the deer of the seven lakes are not enough to fill the kitchen." He believed that humans, endowed with divine essence, should not be bound by conventional norms and should enjoy life fully. He preferred strong, undiluted wine. His peers often teased him and called him "Thirsty Qiang" due to his love for alcohol. When Emperor Wu of Jin ascended the throne, he suddenly remembered Yao Fu and saw him standing at the foot of the steps. The emperor found him unconventional and talented, and appointed him as the governor of Chao Ge.

馥辞曰老羌异域之人远隔山川得游中华已为殊幸请辞朝歌之县长充养马之役时赐美酒以乐余年帝曰朝歌纣之故都地有美酒故使老羌不复呼渴馥于阶下高声而对曰马圉老羌渐染皇化溥天夷貊皆为王臣今若欢酒池之乐更为殷纣之民乎帝抚玉几大悦即迁酒泉太守地有清泉其味若酒馥乘醉而拜受之遂为善政民为立生祠后以府地赐张华犹有草在故茂先镫赋云擢九茎于汉庭美三株于兹馆贵表祥乎金德比名类乎相乱至惠帝元熙元年三株草化为三树枝叶似杨树高五尺以应三杨擅权之事时有杨骏杨瑶杨济三弟兄号曰三杨马圉醉羌所说之验

The Record states: "If one cannot be a person of the middle way, being a wild or strict person is acceptable." Figures like Chunyu and You Meng, who used jest and play to offer advice, exemplify this. As for Yao Fu, his talent, character, and appearance were unlike those of others; with a few words, he subtly criticized, yet his charm was enough to set a standard. In his playful and ingenious manner, he used words to advise and pointed out flaws through objects, speaking without blame, and indeed he was of the same ilk as Eastern Manqian! The corruption of the heart and mind leads to a taste for harmful indulgences, which is why the *Dao De Jing* warns, "The five flavors dull the taste buds." It is better to find sweetness in pine and beauty in grass, to breathe in mist and clouds, to chew on the dew of heaven, to swiftly transcend a thousand spirits in a moment, to compare dust to an instant. How can one indulge in such pleasures and forget the long view? There are things that differ in their nature but share the same name; only those who fathom the divine and understand the principles can see this clearly. How can one rely on biased words and seek only heretical ideas? The mandate of heaven has signs, and the course of history is destined; how can one blindly believe rumors and point to strange phenomena in small plants? To be submerged by what one hears and believe in its methods, this is indeed a matter of regret.

In the fourth year of Xining (278 AD), a fragrant vegetable garden was established east of Jin City, where many exotic vegetables were cultivated. Among these was a vegetable known as "yunwei," which had three varieties: the purple variety was the most abundant, with a spicy flavor, its roots spreading widely, its leaves dense in spring and summer, its flowers blooming in autumn and its fragrance lingering in winter. Its fruit resembled pearls, coming in five colors, and was most abundant at different times of the year. It was also known by the name "yunzhi." The purple variety was considered the best vegetable, with a spicy flavor; the yellow variety was a medium-quality vegetable, with a sweet flavor; and the green variety was the lowest-quality vegetable, with a salty flavor. These three varieties were often used to fill the imperial diet. Their leaves could be used to wrap food, to provide for ancestral temple sacrifices, and to satisfy hunger and thirst. Palace women collected and carried its stems and leaves, whose fragrance lasted for days. It is recorded that in the *Da Ya* section of the *Shijing* (Book of Songs) it says, "Speak of gathering the wild vegetables," which refers to this type of plant. In the *Grass and Wood Treatise* it is said, "Its fruit is like beans." In ancient times, the two sons of the Duke of Guzhu fled the world, refusing to eat the grain of Zhou, and gathered wild vegetables from Mount Shouyang to eat. Some say it is a plant, possibly a divine type, and the more one learns about it, the more one becomes confused. It can relieve hunger, and its color must be purple. Various scholarly opinions agree on its sound and meaning. When discussing its form and quality, its fragrance and color are detailed. Even when transplanted to a fragrant garden, its fragrance and beauty are unmatched. Therefore, the scent of orchids has substance, and the nature of the object remains unchanged, even when produced in a place far from its native land, it becomes even more fragrant and intense. Like ginger and cinnamon, its spiciness is not due to the place but to its inherent qualities. In this era, it is called a divine vegetable, truly a divine and mysterious plant.

Zhang Hua brewed a nine-fermentation wine, using three types of herbs to soak the yeast and malt. The yeast came from the Western Qiang, and the yeast and malt came from the Northern Hu. Among the Hu people, there was a type of wheat called "pointing-to-the-star wheat," which was harvested in April when the star Mars appeared in the sky. The yeast was soaked in water for three nights until it sprouted, and it was used at dawn when the roosters crowed. People commonly called this wheat "rooster-crowing wheat." Using this wheat to brew wine resulted in a pure and delicious beverage that, when held in the mouth for a long time, could make one's teeth tingle. If one drank too much, it could cause severe digestive issues, leading to the liver and intestines becoming inflamed and deteriorating, and people referred to this as "wine that destroys the intestines." Some say that good wine can provide long-lasting pleasure, but these two sayings, though sounding similar, refer to different things. The people of the neighborhood sang, "Would you rather have a pure wine that destroys the intestines than seek eternal fame like the sun and moon?" This proverb expresses a preference for the immediate pleasure of drinking good wine over the pursuit of lasting glory. It suggests that one should enjoy this fine wine for the moment, rather than seeking to preserve one's spirit for long-term goals. By the end of Emperor Huai's reign, the fields and gardens of the common people were overrun with weeds and thorns, and foxes and rabbits roamed freely. In the first year of the Yuanxi era, the Grand Historian Gao Tang Zhong reported that Mars had entered the region of the North Star, predicting disaster if not avoided early. The emperor then ordered that purple wisteria be planted throughout the inner and outer regions, as well as in the capital's palaces, temples, and gardens, and in the common people's fields, as a protective measure. By the end of the reigns of Liu, Shi, Yao, and Fu, these weeds and thorns had naturally disappeared on their own.

In the first year of the Taikang era of the Jin Dynasty, a white cloud rose over the Ba River and disappeared after three days. Officials reported: "This is a sign that the world will be peaceful." The emperor asked the reason, and they replied: "In the time of Emperor Shun, a yellow cloud appeared in the fields; during the Xia Dynasty, a white cloud covered the capital cities; during the Shang Dynasty, a black cloud covered the forests and swamps. These are all signs of peace and prosperity for the world, and distant lands should offer their unique goods as tribute." Indeed, people from Mount Yu offered 10,000 bolts of fire-washed cloth. The people of the country said: "On Mount Yu, there are ornamented stones that produce fire, with the color of the smoke changing with the seasons, and are called 'Pure Fire.' Unclean clothes, when thrown onto the fire-stones, become clean as if freshly washed." During the time of Emperor Shun, the country offered yellow cloth; at the end of the Han Dynasty, it offered red cloth, which was made into clothing by Liang Ji and called "Red Robe." Historians say: "The single robe is now known as 'sewing the sleeves.'" The characters are different but the pronunciation is the same, and it is unclear which is correct.

Record states: The rise of a ruler or dynasty is marked by auspicious signs, as heaven does not hide such omens, and earth does not hoard treasures. Therefore, divine objects are used to indicate the course of destiny, and stars and clouds are observed to discern virtue. According to the *Zhouguan* (Rites of Zhou), there is an office called Fengxiangshi, who observes auspicious signs and records their meanings. The Jin dynasty, associated with the metal element, saw white clouds arise over the Ba River. The *Shanhaijing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) and the *Yiwuzhi* (Record of Strange Things) describe a creature from the land of Ran Zhou that is born in fire, whose fur can be woven into fabric that cleanses itself when burned. Since these two accounts differ, both are recorded here. The country of Yincheng presented a five-legged beast, resembling a lion, and a thousand strings of jade coins, each string containing ten coins, with the characters "Tian Shou Yong Ji" (Long Life and Eternal Good Fortune) inscribed on them. When asked the envoy about the transformation of the five-legged beast, he replied: "There are people in the east who can detach parts of their bodies. They send their head flying to the South Sea, their left hand to the Eastern Mountain, and their right hand to the Western Marsh, leaving only their lower body and two legs. By evening, their head returns to their shoulder, and their hands are carried away by the wind to the land of Xuanzhou, where they transform into the legs of the five-legged beast, with each finger becoming a leg.
